They propose SAGEAgent, a large language model-based agent equipped with clinical translation tools, an episodic memory for retrieving similar past cases, and a semantic memory that accumulates reusable decision patterns, allowing it to learn and refine acquisition strategies over time. Experiments on a glioma cohort integrating multiple public datasets and spanning four diagnostic modalities show that SAGEAgent matches competitive survival prediction accuracy while substantially reducing the average diagnostic burden compared to approaches that acquire all modalities. The work demonstrates that an experience-driven agent can actively reason about when additional diagnostic information is clinically justified rather than passively accommodating missing data.
items.citation: Chongyu Qu, Can Cui, Zhengyi Lu et al.. SAGEAgent: A Self-Evolving Agent for Cost-Aware Modality Acquisition in Multimodal Survival Prediction. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Seeing is Free, Speaking is Not: Uncovering the True Energy Bottleneck in Edge VLM Inference
items.summary: This paper investigated where energy is actually consumed during vision-language model inference on edge hardware, challenging the prevailing assumption that visual processing represents the dominant computational cost. The researchers conducted systematic energy profiling across multiple models, architecture families, input resolutions, and two edge hardware platforms, measuring power draw and inference time under varying conditions. They found that average inference power remains essentially constant regardless of input resolution, image complexity, or prompt type, meaning all energy variation stems from differences in inference time rather than power consumption. The critical bottleneck they identified is output token generation, which costs an order of magnitude more time per token than input processing due to the asymmetry between prefill and decode phases, making output length the dominant driver of both latency and energy. Consequently, they demonstrate that visual token pruning approaches can save only a small fraction of total energy at best, while controlling output length can reduce energy consumption by the vast majority, with this effect growing stronger at larger model scales.
items.citation: Junfei Zhan, Haoxun Shen, Mingang Guo et al.. Seeing is Free, Speaking is Not: Uncovering the True Energy Bottleneck in Edge VLM Inference. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Multiple cross displacement amplification coupled with a lateral flow biosensor for ultra-rapid and highly sensitive detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> in neonatal sepsis.
items.summary: The paper studied rapid detection of *Escherichia coli* as a causative pathogen in neonatal sepsis, where conventional culture methods are slow and insufficiently sensitive. The researchers developed a multiple cross-displacement amplification assay coupled with a lateral flow biosensor targeting the *uidA* gene, using a set of ten primers optimized to run at a fixed temperature for forty minutes. The assay achieved femtogram-level sensitivity with no cross-reactivity against non-*E. coli* strains, and the complete workflow from nucleic acid extraction to visual readout was completed within approximately one hour. Clinical validation using blood and cerebrospinal fluid specimens from neonates with suspected sepsis showed complete agreement with quantitative PCR and substantially higher positivity rates than conventional culture. The authors conclude that this method offers a rapid, sensitive, and operationally simple approach well suited to point-of-care and resource-limited settings for early diagnosis of neonatal *E. coli* infection.
items.citation: Xiao R, Zou P, Zhou J et al.. Multiple cross displacement amplification coupled with a lateral flow biosensor for ultra-rapid and highly sensitive detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> in neonatal sepsis.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: Simultaneous Dual-Gene Detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> O157:H7 Based on a CRISPR/Cas13-Mediated Biosensor.
items.summary: This study addressed the need for rapid, accurate detection of *Escherichia coli* O157:H7, a foodborne pathogen responsible for significant public health risks. The researchers developed a one-pot CRISPR/Cas13-based biosensor that simultaneously targets two key genetic markers characteristic of this serotype, exploiting the distinct collateral cleavage preferences of two different Cas13 proteins to create dual fluorescence channels. Target amplification, transcription, and CRISPR-mediated detection were all integrated into a single reaction tube, removing the requirement for complex instrumentation. The biosensor achieved high sensitivity at a low detection limit using recombinase polymerase amplification, and its performance in analyzing fecal samples from a mouse infection model was consistent with standard PCR results. The approach offers improved serotype discrimination and specificity compared to conventional CRISPR diagnostics, presenting a simple, rapid, and field-deployable platform suitable for food safety monitoring, clinical diagnostics, and infectious disease surveillance.
items.citation: He Y, Lin X, Zhang X et al.. Simultaneous Dual-Gene Detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> O157:H7 Based on a CRISPR/Cas13-Mediated Biosensor.. JACS Au. 2026

items.title: Target-to-signal conversion and spatial enrichment cascade boost CRISPR/Cas12a biosensing for trace-level pathogen detection.
items.summary: This study developed a biosensor for detecting *Salmonella typhimurium*, a major cause of foodborne illness, by integrating DNAzyme-catalyzed signal amplification with CRISPR/Cas12a technology. The approach works by using DNAzymes to generate single-stranded DNA that triggers hybridization chain reaction (HCR) amplification across three hairpin structures, creating branched DNA nanowires bearing PAM sequences that are subsequently cleaved by CRISPR/Cas12a to restore a quenched fluorescence signal. This cascade of target-to-signal conversion and spatial enrichment achieved an extremely low detection limit with a broad linear detection range, enabling trace-level pathogen quantification. The biosensor demonstrated rapid response, straightforward operation, and high sensitivity under optimized conditions. The authors also propose that the platform is adaptable for detecting other foodborne pathogens by substituting the recognition module and primer sequences.
items.venue: Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications
items.citation: Liu Y, Yue X, Li B et al.. Target-to-signal conversion and spatial enrichment cascade boost CRISPR/Cas12a biosensing for trace-level pathogen detection.. Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications. 2026

items.title: Graphene foam biosensors for molecular diagnostics of antimicrobial resistance and emerging pathogen threats.
items.summary: This paper developed an electrochemical biosensor designed for rapid, point-of-care detection of antimicrobial resistance genes and emerging pathogens. The researchers constructed disposable graphene foam electrodes functionalized with pyrene-modified peptide nucleic acid probes, using π-π stacking interactions to immobilize the probes without chemically altering the electrode surface. A key innovation was a heat-assisted functionalization protocol that reduced preparation time dramatically while maintaining sensor performance. The biosensor achieved amplification-free and label-free detection of a colistin resistance gene as well as a carbapenem resistance gene simply by swapping the probe sequence, demonstrating straightforward retargetability. The platform showed strong selectivity against non-complementary sequences, reproducible performance across independently fabricated devices, and detection limits in the picomolar range for bacterial plasmid samples, supporting its potential for scalable decentralized diagnostics.
items.citation: Wang KY, Schulze H, Lozano Sanchez P et al.. Graphene foam biosensors for molecular diagnostics of antimicrobial resistance and emerging pathogen threats.. Biosensors & bioelectronics. 2026

items.title: Advanced biosensing strategies for high-risk foodborne pathogens: a comprehensive review of <i>Salmonella</i> and <i>Listeria monocytogenes</i>.
items.summary: This review examines advances in biosensor technology specifically targeting two major foodborne pathogens, *Salmonella* spp. and *Listeria monocytogenes*, which are among the leading causes of foodborne illness and mortality globally. Rather than broadly surveying biosensing across many pathogens, the authors provide a pathogen-specific comparison of bioreceptor development and transducer choices for these two organisms. A particular focus is placed on how artificial intelligence tools can accelerate biosensor design, optimization, and detection decision-making for these pathogens, which the authors claim represents a novel contribution to the literature. The review also critically evaluates which biosensing strategies hold genuine potential for scalability, portability, and practical deployment in food industry settings, addressing the persistent gap between laboratory research and real-world implementation. Taken together, the review is framed as a practical roadmap intended to guide future biosensor research toward field-ready, industry-relevant solutions for these high-priority pathogens.
items.citation: Gumush SE, Al-Attar R, Sandbecker E et al.. Advanced biosensing strategies for high-risk foodborne pathogens: a comprehensive review of <i>Salmonella</i> and <i>Listeria monocytogenes</i>.. Food chemistry. Molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: Deep learning integrated plasmonic electrochemical sensing for fast and accurate pathogen detection.
items.summary: This paper investigated the integration of plasmonic electrochemical biosensing with deep learning algorithms to achieve rapid and accurate detection of bacterial pathogens. The researchers developed a nanostructured biosensor based on gold-nanoparticle graphene oxide hybrid nanocomposites that exploit localized surface plasmon resonance to enhance charge-transfer kinetics and electrochemical response, targeting Escherichia coli, Salmonella typhimurium, and Staphylococcus aureus. Detection was achieved using very small sample volumes in under ten minutes, with extremely low limits of detection for all three pathogens. Raw voltammetric signals were processed through a combined MobileNet-Transformer and Gated Recurrent Unit deep neural network pipeline, where the MobileNet-Transformer extracted spectral features and the GRU component suppressed temporal noise. The integrated system outperformed baseline convolutional and recurrent neural network models in classification accuracy and discriminative performance, demonstrating a scalable approach to AI-assisted biosensing suitable for food safety, environmental monitoring, and point-of-care diagnostics on portable, low-cost hardware.
items.citation: Mansour S, Negm N, Alhashmi AA et al.. Deep learning integrated plasmonic electrochemical sensing for fast and accurate pathogen detection.. Scientific reports. 2026

items.title: PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis
items.summary: This paper introduces a topological machine learning framework called PHINN-EEG for analyzing EEG signals recorded during dreaming, addressing limitations of conventional approaches that rely on power spectral density and statistical moment features. The method applies sliding-window Takens delay embeddings and Vietoris-Rips filtrations to multichannel EEG data to extract Dynamic Betti Curves, which capture the geometric and topological structure of neural activity rather than its spectral energy content. These topological features are combined with topology-conditioned flow matching for dream-state EEG synthesis, and the framework also proposes candidate Betti transition archetypes as hypothetical links between topological patterns and phenomenological dream report categories. The authors analytically project that their approach will outperform existing spectral and statistical benchmarks on a large open-access dream database, though these performance claims are presented as targets pending empirical validation rather than confirmed experimental results. The work is framed as a conceptual paradigm shift from energy-based to phase-space geometry-based neural signal analysis, with proposed applications in brain-computer interface dream monitoring.
items.citation: Ren Takahashi, Emre Yusuf, Jayabrata Bhaduri. PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Impact of Benign Connectivity Variations on Intrusion Detection for Encrypted OPC UA Traffic in Industrial Private 5G Networks
items.summary: This paper examined how machine learning-based intrusion detection systems behave when monitoring encrypted industrial communications under realistic private 5G network conditions, specifically focusing on OPC UA traffic. The researchers investigated whether normal, benign connectivity variations in these networks could adversely affect detection system performance. Their experiments revealed that legitimate connectivity events cause a noticeable increase in false positive alerts even when no actual attacks are occurring, and that elevated anomaly scores from the detection systems frequently coincide with control-plane activity associated with these events. The findings underscore that control-plane context must be taken into account when interpreting intrusion detection outputs in industrial private 5G deployments to avoid misclassifying normal network behavior as malicious.
items.citation: Song Son Ha, Florian Foerster, Henry Beuster et al.. Impact of Benign Connectivity Variations on Intrusion Detection for Encrypted OPC UA Traffic in Industrial Private 5G Networks. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Identifying and characterizing extragalactic circum-CBC exoplanets with future gravitational-wave detectors
items.summary: This paper investigates whether exoplanets orbiting compact binary coalescences outside our galaxy could be detected using future gravitational wave observatories. The approach relies on extracting information about the centre-of-mass motion of a compact binary system — such as a binary neutron star or binary black hole — as it wobbles around the barycentre it shares with an orbiting planet, with this motion leaving a characteristic imprint on the emitted gravitational wave signal. The authors evaluated the sensitivity of next-generation detectors to this effect across a range of planetary orbital parameters, including both circular and eccentric orbits. They found that for a substantial portion of the parameter space considered, future detectors could not only identify the presence of such a circum-compact-binary exoplanet but also recover its mass to within an order-unity factor at meaningful statistical confidence, albeit with a degeneracy from the unknown orbital inclination. This work constitutes a proof-of-principle demonstration that gravitational wave observations may open an entirely new channel for discovering the first extragalactic exoplanets.
items.citation: Avinash Tiwari, Aditya Vijaykumar, Shasvath J. Kapadia et al.. Identifying and characterizing extragalactic circum-CBC exoplanets with future gravitational-wave detectors. arXiv. 2026

items.title: HD 148797: A bright F-type star with two moderate-period low-density sub-Jovian planets. Compact multi-planet architectures are common in the Neptunian savanna
items.summary: The paper reports the confirmation and characterization of two transiting sub-Jovian planets orbiting the bright F-type star HD 148797, using photometric data from the TESS and CHEOPS space missions. The two planets have orbital periods near a golden-mean ratio and nearly identical radii and masses, with both exhibiting very low bulk densities that place them among the sparse population of planets in the so-called Neptunian savanna. Planetary masses were derived from anti-correlated transit-timing variations rather than radial velocities, yielding a photodynamical solution consistent with a dynamically cold, compact multi-planet architecture. A broader survey of known savanna systems reveals that multi-planet configurations are common in this regime, with most pairs having period ratios below three, pointing toward smooth disk-driven migration rather than disruptive high-eccentricity tidal pathways as the dominant formation route. The system's brightness and its hosting of two co-evolved, nearly twin planets also make it an attractive candidate for comparative atmospheric studies.
items.citation: J. M. Almenara, A. Castro-González, R. Mardling et al.. HD 148797: A bright F-type star with two moderate-period low-density sub-Jovian planets. Compact multi-planet architectures are common in the Neptunian savanna. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models
items.summary: This paper investigated how vision-language models have progressed over a decade in their ability to describe complex social scenes, addressing gaps in prior evaluations that relied on simple imagery and limited human benchmarks. The researchers introduced a new dataset of images depicting complex social interactions and behaviors, then systematically evaluated nine models spanning from early approaches to modern multimodal large language models, alongside human descriptions, using a gold-standard accuracy measure and a taxonomy of five visual-cognitive error types. They found that earlier models performed substantially worse than even the lowest-ranked human describers on complex scenes, while modern multimodal large language models reached accuracy levels comparable to the highest-ranked human descriptions. Notably, modern models have largely closed the performance gap between simple and complex scene description, and have nearly eliminated most error categories, with the exception of spatial dependence errors reflecting differences in which image regions models versus humans attend to. The analysis also revealed that object detection, recognition, and hallucination errors carry the greatest impact on overall scene description accuracy.
items.citation: Shravan Murlidaran, Miguel P. Eckstein. Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models. arXiv. 2026

items.title: VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents
items.summary: This paper investigated whether large language model-based agents could autonomously discover and exploit vulnerabilities in Internet of Things systems, addressing the lack of scalable security testing approaches for constrained IoT devices. The researchers developed a multi-agent framework called VEXAIoT that pairs a vulnerability detection agent with an attack execution agent, enabling automated reconnaissance, attack planning, and exploit execution using offensive security tools. The system was evaluated across ten attack scenarios drawn from OWASP IoT vulnerability categories within two established testbed environments, IoTGoat and Metasploitable. Results demonstrated very high overall attack success rates with low computational overhead, with most attacks completing in under two minutes. The findings suggest that LLM-driven multi-agent frameworks hold considerable promise for automating IoT vulnerability assessment and offensive security workflows in controlled settings.
items.citation: Katherine Swinea, Kshitiz Aryal, Lopamudra Praharaj et al.. VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Etiology of severe acute respiratory infections among adults in northern Thailand using multiplex PCR: A post-COVID-19 surveillance study (2023-2024).
items.summary: This study investigated the causes of severe acute respiratory infections among hospitalized adults in northern Thailand during the post-COVID-19 pandemic period. Researchers conducted a retrospective analysis of respiratory samples from adult patients admitted to a regional hospital in Chiang Mai with SARI symptoms, testing specimens using multiplex real-time PCR capable of detecting a broad panel of respiratory pathogens. Nearly half of patients tested positive for at least one respiratory pathogen, with adenovirus and rhinovirus emerging as the most frequently detected viruses, and co-infections observed in a notable proportion of cases. Seasonal patterns were also identified, with influenza A and rhinovirus peaking in January while adenovirus and enterovirus circulated throughout the study period. The authors conclude that enhanced molecular surveillance and broader rapid diagnostics are needed to reduce empiric antibiotic use and improve evidence-based clinical management in the post-pandemic era.
items.citation: Kham-Kjing N, Kawila R, Tariyo P et al.. Etiology of severe acute respiratory infections among adults in northern Thailand using multiplex PCR: A post-COVID-19 surveillance study (2023-2024).. PloS one. 2026

items.title: VisPan: real-time visualisation of multiplex amplicon-based sequencing panels for rapid syndromic surveillance and pathogen detection.
items.summary: This paper addresses the bioinformatics bottleneck in real-time pathogen detection using Oxford Nanopore Technologies sequencing by presenting a new visualisation tool called VisPan. The tool was adapted from existing RAMPART software to enable real-time data visualisation of multiplex PCR syndromic panels combined with long-read nanopore sequencing, moving from raw data acquisition to taxonomic assignment within minutes. The interface provides dynamic visual tracking of sequencing runs and amplicon coverage, supporting rapid diagnostic decision-making in clinical or field settings. Validation experiments using complex clinical and environmental samples demonstrated that the system reliably and accurately identified all pathogens present. The authors propose VisPan as an accessible, integrated solution for genomic syndromic surveillance and outbreak response.
items.citation: Lechat P, Kwasiborski A, Vincent R et al.. VisPan: real-time visualisation of multiplex amplicon-based sequencing panels for rapid syndromic surveillance and pathogen detection.. Bioinformatics (Oxford, England). 2026

items.title: Performance evaluation of the BioFire respiratory panel 2.1 plus and exploratory analysis of Ct values for respiratory pathogen quantification.
items.summary: The study evaluated the diagnostic performance of the BioFire FilmArray RP2.1 plus, a multiplex PCR panel capable of detecting twenty respiratory pathogens, and explored whether its cycle threshold values could serve as a semi-quantitative measure of pathogen load. Using a retrospective design, researchers tested clinical specimens on the BioFire platform and compared results against established comparator assays, generating a large set of PCR results for qualitative and quantitative analysis. The BioFire panel demonstrated excellent qualitative agreement with comparator methods, with most discordant results occurring in samples containing low pathogen loads as indicated by high cycle threshold values. Exploratory quantitative analyses revealed strong correlations between BioFire-derived cycle threshold values and those from reference assays, suggesting a reproducible cross-platform relationship. The authors conclude that while the panel performs well for routine qualitative detection, the potential utility of its cycle threshold values for semi-quantitative assessment warrants cautious interpretation given the exploratory nature of the analysis and inherent limitations of comparing across different platforms.
items.venue: European journal of clinical microbiology & infectious diseases : official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology
items.citation: Vitale A, Mahler Z, Loup V et al.. Performance evaluation of the BioFire respiratory panel 2.1 plus and exploratory analysis of Ct values for respiratory pathogen quantification.. European journal of clinical microbiology & infectious diseases : official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology. 2026

items.title: Pneumococcal Detection and Bacterial Co-Detection in Children After COVID-19: A Two-Year Multiplex PCR Study.
items.summary: The study investigated pediatric pneumococcal respiratory infections and bacterial co-detection patterns in the two years immediately following the COVID-19 pandemic, during which non-pharmaceutical interventions had disrupted normal respiratory pathogen circulation. Researchers retrospectively analyzed respiratory specimens from children aged up to eighteen years using a multiplex real-time PCR panel targeting seven bacterial pathogens at a tertiary pediatric hospital in Romania across 2022 and 2023. They found a substantial rise in pneumococcal positivity over the two-year period, with detections increasing more than fourfold in absolute numbers and the positivity rate more than tripling. Notably, co-detection of *Streptococcus pneumoniae* alongside *Haemophilus influenzae* increased significantly, while detection of pneumococcus alone declined, and community-acquired pneumonia diagnoses rose correspondingly. The authors conclude that these findings reflect a post-pandemic rebound in pediatric pneumococcal disease and shifting co-infection patterns, underscoring the value of syndromic multiplex PCR testing for rapid diagnostics and guiding antimicrobial stewardship.
items.citation: Stavăr-Matei L, Țocu L, Nechita A et al.. Pneumococcal Detection and Bacterial Co-Detection in Children After COVID-19: A Two-Year Multiplex PCR Study.. Biomedicines. 2026

items.title: Post-pandemic resurgence and co-detectiondynamics of respiratory pathogens revealed by syndromic multiplex PCR.
items.summary: This study investigated the distribution, co-detection patterns, seasonal variation, and sequential infections of respiratory pathogens in patients with suspected respiratory tract infections at a tertiary-care hospital in Türkiye during the post-pandemic period. Using the BioFire FilmArray Respiratory Panel, researchers retrospectively analysed hundreds of respiratory samples from patients over approximately one year. At least one pathogen was detected in just over half of all samples, with Rhinovirus/Enterovirus being the most frequently identified agent, followed by influenza A, adenovirus, SARS-CoV-2, RSV, and Mycoplasma pneumoniae. Co-detections occurred in roughly one-fifth of positive samples and were dominated by Rhinovirus/Enterovirus, while sequential sample analysis suggested that many apparent co-infections may instead reflect residual nucleic acid from recently cleared infections rather than true simultaneous co-infections. The findings confirm high etiological diversity, pronounced seasonality, and the clinical value of syndromic multiplex PCR-based diagnostics for guiding patient management in the post-pandemic era.
items.citation: Aygar IS. Post-pandemic resurgence and co-detectiondynamics of respiratory pathogens revealed by syndromic multiplex PCR.. BMC microbiology. 2026

items.title: OAM-Enabled Holographic MIMO Communications with Stacked Intelligent Metasurfaces
items.summary: This paper investigates how stacked intelligent metasurfaces can be used to generate and receive orbital angular momentum modes in holographic multiple-input multiple-output communication systems operating in the radiative near-field. The researchers analyzed the use of multilayer programmable metasurfaces at both transmitter and receiver ends to perform analog electromagnetic wave processing, enabling flexible wavefront synthesis with low complexity and energy efficiency. A correlation-driven optimization algorithm was developed to maximize the accuracy of orbital angular momentum beam reconstruction in practical discrete architectures. The study found a fundamental decoupling between antenna aperture size, which determines the supported mode orders, and the depth of the metasurface stack, which controls crosstalk suppression between modes. Results demonstrate that properly designed stacked intelligent metasurface systems achieve robust spatial multiplexing with nearly balanced per-mode capacities and graceful performance degradation over varying link distances, without requiring continuous phase reconfiguration.
items.citation: Giulia Torcolacci, Davide Dardari. OAM-Enabled Holographic MIMO Communications with Stacked Intelligent Metasurfaces. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Report on the Advanced Linear Collider Study Group (ALEGRO) Workshop 2026
items.summary: This report summarizes discussions from an international workshop dedicated to advanced novel accelerators, focusing on the role of wakefield acceleration technologies in future particle and high-energy physics applications. The workshop brought together the global community to review progress in plasma-based and structure-based wakefield acceleration, with particular emphasis on a design study for a future energy-frontier linear collider at the tens-of-TeV scale. Sessions addressed a broad range of topics including accelerator concepts, enabling technologies, near-term applications such as injectors and fixed-target experiments, plasma-based Higgs factory proposals, and proton-driven plasma wakefield acceleration. Notable experimental progress was highlighted in free-electron laser lasing achieved with plasma-accelerated electron beams, demonstrating meaningful advances in beam quality. The workshop also examined operational challenges for user facilities and the increasing application of artificial intelligence and machine learning to accelerator optimization and control, concluding with an overview of the current status and future prospects of the field.
items.citation: L. Verra, P. Muggli, B. Cross et al.. Report on the Advanced Linear Collider Study Group (ALEGRO) Workshop 2026. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Inference of large scale relational state processes
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of modeling relational states—persistent relationships such as friendships or collaborations—that can transition among more than two states over time, a scenario that most existing methods cannot handle. The authors developed a continuous-time framework in which each network edge evolves through multiple states driven by covariates representing anchoring (current-state) and pulling (target-state) mechanisms, accommodating both linear and smooth nonlinear effects. To handle different data collection regimes, they derived a Cox-type partial likelihood with nested case-control sampling for full event histories and an ODE-based likelihood formulation for panel data, the latter being especially efficient for binary-state models. Simulation studies demonstrated accurate parameter recovery, and an application to adolescent friendship data reproduced established findings while yielding notable computational advantages. The framework generalizes classical network effects to multi-state ties while remaining interpretable and scalable to large, complex network designs.
items.citation: Rūta Juozaitienė, Ernst C. Wit. Inference of large scale relational state processes. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Optical Fourier Surfaces for Free-Space Computer-Generated Holography
items.summary: This paper introduces optical Fourier surfaces as a platform for free-space computer-generated holography, addressing the longstanding limitation that conventional lithographic fabrication produces discretized surface profiles poorly matched to the continuous wave nature of light. The researchers leveraged thermal scanning-probe lithography to fabricate arbitrarily shaped, continuous (grayscale) nanostructured surfaces, enabling direct application of Fourier optics design principles. Three complementary design strategies were demonstrated: an analytical linear approach using sinusoidal lenses and gratings as building blocks, an iterative Fourier-transform algorithm to enhance diffraction efficiency, and a machine-learning-based inverse design method capable of producing wavelength-multiplexed holograms that reconstruct distinct images at a predefined plane. Together, these approaches show that continuous surface control unlocks more intuitive and flexible hologram design compared to discretized fabrication methods. The work positions optical Fourier surfaces as a versatile tool for photonic applications, especially where rapid prototyping is important.
items.citation: Y. M. Glauser, C. -Y. Shen, D. B. Seda et al.. Optical Fourier Surfaces for Free-Space Computer-Generated Holography. arXiv. 2026

items.title: When Does Order Flow Matter? State-Dependent L2 Liquidity-State Transitions in Crypto Futures
items.summary: This paper investigates how order book liquidity states and order flow interact to predict liquidity regime transitions in cryptocurrency futures markets, specifically asking when and whether order flow carries predictive value beyond what the existing market microstructure state already reveals. The researchers constructed a supervised classification task focused on discrete liquidity-state transitions using top-of-book depth data, trade-flow records, and macro-event timing windows, evaluated through rigorous out-of-sample rolling folds with blocked permutation tests to guard against spurious findings. They found that the pre-event liquidity state is the dominant predictive signal, with a coarse state baseline outperforming interpretable continuous-feature logit models, and a shallow nonlinear model adding only a modest further gain. Order flow contributes additional predictive value only when layered on top of the liquidity state model rather than replacing it, and this contribution is not consistent across assets: it is robustly present for ETH across multiple liquidity regimes but largely absent for BTC. The authors conclude by proposing a state-first design principle for microstructure modeling and offer their liquidity-state transition framework as a baseline that more complex approaches, including reinforcement learning or language-model-based systems, should demonstrably surpass before claiming credit for their added complexity.
items.citation: Joohyoung Jeon. When Does Order Flow Matter? State-Dependent L2 Liquidity-State Transitions in Crypto Futures. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Bidirectional Resource Scheduling for Disaggregated and Asynchronous RL Post-Training
items.summary: This paper addresses inefficiencies in reinforcement learning post-training for large language models, where disaggregated and asynchronous architectures still leave rollout and training resources sitting idle across diverse hardware and workload configurations. The authors developed BiDiRL, a hybrid time-space multiplexing system built around three components: a hot-switch runtime for rapidly reassigning resources between rollout and training, a static scheduling-aware planner that balances the durations of these two stages at a coarse level, and a bidirectional scheduler that dynamically reallocates idle resources to whichever stage is currently the bottleneck. By enabling the bottleneck stage to temporarily borrow resources from the other pool at fine granularity, the system reduces the idle periods that limit throughput. Evaluated across a range of models, datasets, and workloads on two multi-GPU testbeds, BiDiRL achieved substantial throughput improvements over existing RL training frameworks without degrading convergence behavior.
items.citation: Tan Zhiqiang, Wang Maoxin, Wang Sijie et al.. Bidirectional Resource Scheduling for Disaggregated and Asynchronous RL Post-Training. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Ag-rich Ag-Au nanoshells based lateral flow immunoassay for rapid and sensitive detection of bovine viral diarrhea virus.
items.citation: Ding Y, Zhang W, Huang Y et al.. Ag-rich Ag-Au nanoshells based lateral flow immunoassay for rapid and sensitive detection of bovine viral diarrhea virus.. Mikrochimica acta. 2026

items.title: First implementation of a polymer-polymer aqueous two-phase system to improve lateral-flow immunoassay sensitivity via dextranase integration on paper.
items.summary: This paper investigated whether a polymer-polymer aqueous two-phase system (ATPS) could be integrated with lateral-flow immunoassays (LFAs) to improve their sensitivity, addressing a longstanding limitation of these widely used point-of-care diagnostic tools. The researchers used a poly(ethylene glycol)-dextran ATPS to preconcentrate hydrophilic protein targets into the dextran-rich phase, but had to overcome the problem of that phase's high viscosity preventing adequate fluid flow on the LFA strip. To solve this, they introduced the enzyme dextranase to degrade dextran and reduce viscosity, optimizing enzymatic and fluid-flow conditions before combining the full system. Testing with the protein transferrin spiked in artificial saliva demonstrated a tenfold improvement in the limit of detection compared to a conventional LFA, and this improvement was maintained when dextranase was dehydrated directly onto the paper strip to simplify liquid handling. This work represents the first successful coupling of a polymer-polymer ATPS with an LFA and opens the door to a new class of sample preconcentration strategies for point-of-care diagnostics.
items.venue: Analytical sciences : the international journal of the Japan Society for Analytical Chemistry
items.citation: Nicklen FD, Hadeed CH, Zhang F et al.. First implementation of a polymer-polymer aqueous two-phase system to improve lateral-flow immunoassay sensitivity via dextranase integration on paper.. Analytical sciences : the international journal of the Japan Society for Analytical Chemistry. 2026

items.title: Lateral flow immunoassay detection of soluble AXL for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma diagnosis.
items.summary: This paper investigated the development of a rapid diagnostic test for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, a cancer with poor prognosis largely due to the lack of reliable early-detection biomarkers. The researchers developed and optimized a quantitative colorimetric lateral flow immunoassay using gold nanoparticle conjugates to detect soluble AXL, a cleaved form of the AXL receptor tyrosine kinase that circulates in plasma, systematically refining reagent concentrations, buffer composition, and membrane selection to improve sensitivity and reproducibility. Clinical evaluation showed the assay could accurately distinguish PDAC patients from healthy controls, with results validated against standard enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay measurements. The authors claim this represents the first quantitative lateral flow immunoassay for soluble AXL detection in plasma, offering a portable, cost-effective point-of-care diagnostic approach. They propose this method has potential to improve early PDAC diagnosis, while noting that future work will expand patient cohorts and further enhance assay sensitivity and clinical applicability.
items.citation: Vázquez-Bellón N, Rodríguez-Núñez M, Martínez-Bosch N et al.. Lateral flow immunoassay detection of soluble AXL for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma diagnosis.. Talanta. 2026

items.title: Dual-enhancement lateral flow immunoassay <i>via</i> gold-decorated ferritin nanoprobes and SpyTag/SpyCatcher site-directed conjugation.
items.summary: This paper addressed the limited sensitivity of conventional lateral flow immunoassays, which arises from weak optical signals, randomly oriented antibodies, and inconsistent nanoprobe fabrication. The researchers developed a dual-enhancement approach combining gold-decorated ferritin nanocages with SpyTag/SpyCatcher chemistry, where engineered ferritin nanocages displaying SpyTag peptides served as templates for controlled gold nanocluster growth while retaining functional sites for subsequent antibody attachment. The gold deposition produced highly uniform core-shell nanostructures with amplified optical signals, and the SpyTag/SpyCatcher system enabled covalent, site-directed antibody conjugation that dramatically improved antigen-binding capacity compared to random adsorption. Using human chorionic gonadotropin as a model target, the optimized assay achieved a detection limit far lower than commercial gold nanoparticle-based strips, with reliable performance in urine samples demonstrated by strong spike-recovery rates. The authors conclude that this synergistic strategy offers a broadly applicable route to substantially enhancing the sensitivity of point-of-care diagnostic platforms.
items.venue: Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications
items.citation: Yuan X, Zhu X, Xu Y et al.. Dual-enhancement lateral flow immunoassay <i>via</i> gold-decorated ferritin nanoprobes and SpyTag/SpyCatcher site-directed conjugation.. Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications. 2026

items.title: Advances in spherical nanoparticle-labeled lateral flow immunoassays in the field of clinical detection.
items.summary: This review examined the use of spherical nanoparticle labels in lateral flow immunoassays for clinical diagnostic applications, with a focus on point-of-care testing contexts. The authors conducted a bibliometric analysis of relevant literature spanning a decade to map research trends, then systematically summarized preparation and optimization strategies for diverse spherical nanomaterial labels including noble metal nanoparticles, fluorescent nanoparticles, nanozyme-active nanoparticles, surface-enhanced Raman scattering active nanoparticles, and magnetic nanoparticles. The review also addressed how multimodal signal strategies—combining colorimetric, fluorescent, Raman, magnetic, and photothermal readouts—can synergistically enhance diagnostic performance, and how surface functionalization improves detection sensitivity and specificity. The authors argue that such spherical nanomaterials offer particular advantages in clinical translatability and commercialization compared to other label formats. The review is positioned as a foundational resource for advancing precision diagnostics and the development of personalized, intelligent home-based health monitoring systems.
items.citation: Zhang Y, He H, Wang J et al.. Advances in spherical nanoparticle-labeled lateral flow immunoassays in the field of clinical detection.. Journal of materials chemistry. B. 2026

items.title: Simplified Point-of-Care Testing for Human Pythiosis: Development of a Whole-Blood-Based Lateral Flow Immunoassay.
items.summary: The paper studied the development and clinical evaluation of a rapid point-of-care diagnostic test for human pythiosis, a life-threatening infection caused by *Pythium insidiosum* that is often diagnosed too late due to slow conventional culture methods. The researchers created a lateral flow immunoassay using secretory protein antigens from *P. insidiosum* conjugated to gold nanoparticles, designed to detect anti-*P. insidiosum* antibodies directly from whole blood within five minutes. The assay was analytically validated for sensitivity, cross-reactivity, and interference from common anticoagulants and adverse sample conditions, then clinically evaluated against a panel of confirmed positive and negative serum specimens. The test demonstrated high diagnostic performance, with perfect sensitivity and strong specificity, and showed no cross-reactivity with other infections or interference from whole blood components. The authors conclude that this strip test offers a simple, rapid serodiagnostic tool suitable for screening and triage of suspected pythiosis, especially in resource-limited settings where laboratory infrastructure is unavailable.
items.citation: Szekely J, Saelai K, Youngchim S et al.. Simplified Point-of-Care Testing for Human Pythiosis: Development of a Whole-Blood-Based Lateral Flow Immunoassay.. Diagnostics (Basel, Switzerland). 2026

items.title: PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis
items.summary: This paper introduces a topological framework for analyzing EEG signals recorded during dream states, aiming to improve upon existing spectral and statistical approaches to dream detection. The authors apply sliding-window Takens delay embeddings and Vietoris-Rips filtrations to multichannel EEG data to extract what they term Dynamic Betti Curves, which capture the geometric and topological structure of neural activity in phase space rather than simply its spectral energy content. These topological features are combined with a topology-conditioned rectified flow model for dream-state EEG synthesis, and the framework is benchmarked against standard power spectral density and catch22 feature baselines on an open-access subset of the DREAM database. The authors analytically project that their approach will outperform current state-of-the-art classification performance and propose candidate Betti transition archetypes as exploratory hypotheses linking topological signatures to phenomenological dream report categories. Notably, the abstract indicates that the performance improvements are projected rather than empirically demonstrated, and the proposed archetypes are explicitly presented as pending empirical validation.
items.citation: Ren Takahashi, Emre Yusuf, Jayabrata Bhaduri. PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Kleene Algebra with Transitive Commutativity Conditions
items.summary: This paper investigates the decidability of the equational theory of Kleene algebra extended with commutativity conditions, which are equations asserting that certain pairs of program actions can be freely reordered. The central question is: for which binary relations on constant symbols does this extended theory remain decidable? The authors prove a complete characterization, showing that the equational theory of Kleene algebra with commutativity conditions is decidable if and only if the commutativity relation is transitive, mirroring a previously known result for regular languages modulo commutativity. On the negative side, they demonstrate that when the relation is not transitive, even the universality problem becomes undecidable, and on the positive side, they show that for transitive relations the equational theories of Kleene algebra with and without a Kleene star axiom coincide. This resolves a long-standing open problem in the algebraic theory of programs, completing a picture that had been only partially addressed by prior undecidability results.
items.citation: Han Xu, Chenyu Zhou, Zachary Kincaid et al.. Kleene Algebra with Transitive Commutativity Conditions. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Generalized skew-gradient embedding for thermodynamically consistent systems
items.summary: This paper extends the skew-gradient embedding framework, which reformulates thermodynamically consistent systems as generalized gradient flows by embedding zero-energy contributions in a skew-symmetric operator, into a broader family called generalized skew-gradient embeddings. The authors demonstrate that the skew-symmetric operator in this construction is not unique, showing instead that the admissible choices form an affine space, and they characterize how different choices—called gauges—can be selected to minimize a Hilbert–Schmidt norm, regularize approximations, or preserve prescribed invariants. A Jacobi criterion is established as a necessary and sufficient condition for rank-two gauges, which is then applied to yield a finite-dimensional Poisson–GENERIC formulation of the incompressible Navier–Stokes equations on a compatible MAC discretization. For this system, the implicit midpoint rule preserves the rank-two GENERIC structure at the fully discrete level and satisfies an exact discrete energy law. Applied to the coupled Cahn–Hilliard–Navier–Stokes system, the proposed regularized scheme unconditionally dissipates discrete energy, conserves mass, and admits a decoupled implementation.
items.citation: Xuelong Gu, Qi Wang. Generalized skew-gradient embedding for thermodynamically consistent systems. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Generalized Nonlinear Imaginary-Time Evolution
items.summary: This paper studies imaginary-time evolution as an optimization framework and proposes a generalization called nonlinear imaginary-time evolution (NITE) for broader quantum state-preparation tasks beyond ground-state preparation. The authors reframe normalized imaginary-time evolution as a gradient flow with respect to the Fubini-Study metric and extend this perspective to handle more general objective functions. They develop a hardware-efficient variational implementation of NITE and establish its theoretical connection to quantum natural gradient descent, proving local exponential convergence under reasonable assumptions. The method is applied to several tasks including variance minimization in variational eigensolvers, probe-state preparation for quantum sensing, and excited-state preparation using penalty terms. The results demonstrate that NITE consistently outperforms standard gradient descent, positioning it as a broadly useful optimization method for variational quantum algorithms.
items.citation: Chenyu Shi, Hao Wang, Jin-Fu Chen. Generalized Nonlinear Imaginary-Time Evolution. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Numerical Simulation of Turbulent Concentric Annular Pipe Flow using One-Dimensional Turbulence (ODT): Part 2: Heat Transfer
items.summary: This paper investigates turbulent heat transfer in concentric annular pipe flow across a wide parametric range using the one-dimensional turbulence model, a stochastic dimensionally-reduced approach that resolves viscous, conductive, and turbulent transport processes along a representative radial coordinate. The study examines how the radius ratio between the inner and outer walls affects thermal statistics at moderate and low Prandtl numbers, operating up to highly turbulent flow regimes with a fixed model calibration. A key analytical finding is that the conventional linear expression for near-wall temperature statistics is inadequate, particularly at the curved inner wall, and that a logarithmic law of the wall better describes the passive temperature boundary layer when curvature and finite Reynolds number effects are accounted for. Building on analytical boundary layer profiles derived from the numerical predictions, the authors develop heat transfer scaling relations expressed as a Nusselt number correlation extended to incorporate radius ratio dependence. The results demonstrate that the radius ratio substantially influences thermal statistics at both curved walls and must be considered even at high Reynolds numbers and low Prandtl numbers.
items.citation: Pei-Yun Tsai, Heiko Schmidt, Marten Klein. Numerical Simulation of Turbulent Concentric Annular Pipe Flow using One-Dimensional Turbulence (ODT): Part 2: Heat Transfer. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Entropy-Constrained Machine Learning with Residual Data Augmentation for Modeling Chemical Kinetics
items.summary: This paper presents a machine learning surrogate framework designed to replace expensive direct evaluation of chemical source terms in turbulent combustion simulations. The approach incorporates the second law of thermodynamics as a training constraint by enforcing non-negative entropy production, ensuring that predicted thermochemical states remain physically admissible and improving numerical stability during time integration. The framework is demonstrated on direct numerical simulation of a lean premixed methane-air flame interacting with turbulence, where it accurately reproduces detailed-chemistry results while reducing computational cost by more than an order of magnitude. Additionally, a residual-based synthetic data augmentation strategy is introduced, allowing the construction of new training data from existing simulations so that accurate predictions can be made at new inlet conditions without running additional high-fidelity reference simulations. Together, these developments suggest that thermodynamically constrained machine learning surrogates can serve as reliable and efficient replacements for detailed chemical kinetics in high-fidelity combustion modeling.
items.citation: Okezzi Ukorigho, Opeoluwa Owoyele. Entropy-Constrained Machine Learning with Residual Data Augmentation for Modeling Chemical Kinetics. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Feasibility of integrating a point of care triage test into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique: a qualitative evaluation
items.summary: This qualitative study explored perceptions of integrating a point-of-care triage test measuring interferon-gamma-induced protein 10 into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique, where timely viral load testing remains difficult due to laboratory delays and health system constraints. Researchers conducted multiple rounds of interviews with people living with HIV on antiretroviral therapy, healthcare providers, and HIV programme stakeholders across different health system levels, purposively sampling participants to capture diverse test outcome experiences. Participants broadly valued routine viral load monitoring but described significant access barriers, and the point-of-care triage test was found to be widely acceptable, with same-day results perceived to reduce patient anxiety, support treatment adherence, and facilitate timely clinical decisions. Providers and stakeholders recognized the test's potential to improve monitoring efficiency by identifying clients needing confirmatory viral load testing while reducing unnecessary testing among those likely virally suppressed, though concerns about test accuracy and the need to preserve confirmatory testing were noted. The authors conclude that successful implementation would require clear patient communication, staff training, workflow integration, and quality assurance to effectively embed the test within existing antiretroviral therapy monitoring algorithms and support differentiated care approaches.
items.citation: Myburgh H, Saura-Lázaro A, van den Bogaart E et al.. Feasibility of integrating a point of care triage test into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique: a qualitative evaluation. 2026

items.title: Implementation bottlenecks of near point of care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study.
items.summary: This qualitative study investigated the bottlenecks healthcare workers encountered when implementing near point-of-care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people living with HIV in Tanzania. Using clinic observations and in-depth interviews conducted across multiple time points with purposively selected healthcare workers at Tanzanian intervention sites, the researchers applied a structured thematic framework analysis to identify barriers and facilitators across intervention, provider, organisational, and broader contextual levels. The findings showed that near point-of-care testing improved result turnaround times and enabled same-day counselling, which motivated both staff and patients, with implementation supported by compatibility with existing practices, teamwork, and management commitment. Key challenges included lengthy client wait times, scarcity of physical resources such as rooms and electricity, staff shortages, and delays when samples required transport to nearby laboratories. The authors conclude that realising the full benefits of near point-of-care viral load monitoring requires prioritised investment in staff training, infrastructure, sample handling procedures, and alignment with clinical guidelines.
items.citation: Msoka P, Mtenga A, Maro R et al.. Implementation bottlenecks of near point of care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study.. PloS one. 2026

items.title: Baseline Data on Viral Suppression, Treatment Adherence and Mental Health in Peripartum Girls and Young Women With HIV in Zimbabwe.
items.summary: This study examined viral suppression, treatment adherence, drug exposure, and mental health among peripartum adolescent girls and young women living with HIV in Zimbabwe, a population known to have lower viral suppression rates than older women. Participants aged fifteen to twenty-four years who were in the peripartum period and on tenofovir-based antiretroviral therapy were enrolled in a randomized controlled and observational cohort study, with point-of-care viral load testing, urine tenofovir assays, and validated mental health screening tools administered at baseline. The researchers found high rates of viral suppression at enrollment, with detectable urine tenofovir and higher self-reported adherence being the strongest predictors of viral suppression, while marriage, food security, and financial security were also positively associated. Mental health outcomes were largely favorable, with most participants reporting minimal depression and anxiety, though moderate HIV-related stigma was prevalent in over half the cohort. These baseline findings suggest that dolutegravir-based regimens in this population are associated with encouraging virological outcomes, and that adherence support alongside socioeconomic stability may be key factors in sustaining viral control.
items.venue: Journal of the International AIDS Society
items.citation: Chawana-Mutingwende TD, Ngara B, Mudadi LS et al.. Baseline Data on Viral Suppression, Treatment Adherence and Mental Health in Peripartum Girls and Young Women With HIV in Zimbabwe.. Journal of the International AIDS Society. 2026

items.title: Characterisation of a strontium cold atom source using fluorescence spectroscopy and time-of-flight
items.summary: This paper presents a methodology for characterising a cold strontium atomic beam produced by a two-dimensional magneto-optical trap and delivered via a resonant push beam. The researchers used fluorescence spectroscopy and time-of-flight measurements to extract key beam properties, including transverse velocity spread, longitudinal velocity distributions, beam divergence, and capturable flux. Two probe beam configurations — unidirectional and counter-propagating — were employed to measure flux-per-longitudinal-velocity distributions across a range of push beam saturation parameters, with a simulation-derived correction factor applied to account for differences in photon scattering rates between the two approaches. Integrating these distributions up to an estimated capture velocity for a three-dimensional magneto-optical trap yielded consistent capturable flux values from both probe configurations. The authors present this framework as a broadly applicable tool for characterising cold atomic sources intended for applications such as three-dimensional MOT loading and atom interferometry.
items.citation: Kamran Hussain, Hamza Labiad, Anna L. Marchant et al.. Characterisation of a strontium cold atom source using fluorescence spectroscopy and time-of-flight. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Perturbed utility Markovian traffic equilibrium: theory and computation
items.summary: This paper develops a new framework for large-scale traffic assignment that balances behavioral realism with computational efficiency. The authors construct the perturbed utility Markovian equilibrium framework, grounded in a perturbed utility Markovian choice model that generalizes existing additive random utility approaches by using a convex surplus function to define optimal routing policies. A key advance is that the framework naturally permits unattractive links to receive zero flow without requiring artificial restrictions on choice sets in advance, and it accommodates boundary choice probabilities, undiscounted network loading, and non-separable or asymmetric cost structures through a variational inequality formulation on the dual cost space. For computation, the authors introduce a modified policy iteration method for network loading and a safeguarded accelerated meta-algorithm for equilibrium computation, both proven to be globally convergent. Experiments on benchmark and synthetic networks demonstrate that the framework is highly scalable and robust across a wide range of demand and supply conditions.
items.citation: Rui Yao, Kenan Zhang. Perturbed utility Markovian traffic equilibrium: theory and computation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: A Multi-Scale Optimization Framework for Grid-Integrated Electrolysis
items.summary: This paper studied the economic and operational tradeoffs involved in integrating water electrolyzers into power grids, where dynamic operation for demand response purposes accelerates device degradation over time. The researchers developed a mixed-integer linear program optimization framework that couples short-term operational flexibility decisions with long-term stack replacement scheduling driven by degradation. Using an alkaline water electrolyzer participating in a day-ahead energy market over a multi-decade horizon as a case study, they found that multi-scale optimization can extend stack lifetimes and substantially reduce lifetime electricity costs compared to constant inflexible operation. The framework also reveals key device parameter tradeoffs and raises doubts about whether the commonly cited levelized cost of hydrogen target can be achieved through market arbitrage alone. Overall, the work demonstrates that coordinated multi-scale scheduling captures significant economic value that is largely overlooked in conventional approaches to grid-integrated electrolysis.
items.citation: Kiernan X. Jennings, Victor M. Zavala, Styliani Avraamidou. A Multi-Scale Optimization Framework for Grid-Integrated Electrolysis. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Dispersion Polymerization in an Elastomeric Solvent
items.summary: This paper introduces a new method called Dispersion Polymerization in an Elastomeric Solvent (DiPolES), which adapts the well-established liquid-phase dispersion polymerization process to a solid elastomeric medium. By conducting controlled photoinitiated polymerization of monomers within a crosslinked elastomeric network, the elastic network simultaneously acts as a solvent analog and a physical stabilizer, driving polymerization-induced phase separation in the solid state. Using poly(dimethyl siloxane) as the elastomeric host and methyl methacrylate as a model monomer, the approach produces uniform polymer microparticles with tunable sizes and shapes, including ellipsoidal particles generated simply by applying uniaxial strain during polymerization without any post-processing. Real-time imaging revealed that particle formation proceeds through rapid nucleation at low monomer conversion followed by growth accompanied by cavitation of the surrounding network, with monomer loading controlling particle size and network elasticity governing the transition between isolated particles and heterogeneous clusters. The method generalizes to other monomers and enables high-yield extraction of microparticles, broadening the toolkit for fabricating structured polymeric composites and microparticles beyond the constraints of traditional liquid-phase dispersion polymerization.
items.citation: Senthilkumar Duraivel, Reagan J Dreiling, Tyler E Ball et al.. Dispersion Polymerization in an Elastomeric Solvent. arXiv. 2026

items.title: STEEL: Sparsity-Aware Fused Attention for Energy-Efficient Long-Sequence Inference on AMD's XDNA NPU
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of running large language model attention mechanisms efficiently on neural processing units (NPUs) found in laptop-class system-on-chip devices, motivated by privacy and reliability concerns with cloud-based inference. The authors developed STEEL, the first open-source implementation of FlashAttention targeting XDNA-like NPU architectures, which reformulates attention as a dataflow computation to exploit spatial parallelism and on-chip memory effectively. A key contribution is a sparsity-aware pipeline placement strategy that mitigates load imbalance caused by the causal mask in autoregressive models, reducing synchronization overhead and improving hardware utilization. Evaluated on a commercial AMD laptop SoC, STEEL demonstrated substantial reductions in energy consumption relative to both CPU and GPU baselines, as well as large latency improvements over prior NPU attention implementations on both generations of the XDNA architecture.
items.citation: Victor J. B. Jung, Gagandeep Singh, Joseph Melber et al.. STEEL: Sparsity-Aware Fused Attention for Energy-Efficient Long-Sequence Inference on AMD's XDNA NPU. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Sharing Coefficient-Based Price Signals for Demand Response in Renewable Energy Communities
items.summary: This paper investigated how sharing coefficients in renewable energy communities can be repurposed from retrospective billing tools into forward-looking price signals that guide household electricity demand. The researchers developed a demand response framework that translates dynamic proportional surplus allocation outcomes into day-ahead price signals, with one design that accounts for feeder topology by sharing surplus within each feeder before pooling the remainder, and another that treats the entire community as a single pool. An energy community manager iteratively computes allocations from submitted demand and photovoltaic forecasts, decomposes purchased energy into same-feeder, inter-feeder, and grid-import components, and coordinates household load reshaping through convex optimization. Testing with measured household profiles and AC power flow analysis showed that both designs substantially reduced reverse power flow at the feeder level, with the feeder-aware approach providing a modest additional network benefit by incorporating household location within the low-voltage network. The findings demonstrate that sharing coefficients are not limited to ex-post accounting but can serve as effective operational signals for demand response in photovoltaic-rich low-voltage networks.
items.citation: Alireza Shooshtari, Antonio Pepiciello, Jose Luis Dominguez-Garcia. Sharing Coefficient-Based Price Signals for Demand Response in Renewable Energy Communities. arXiv. 2026

items.title: 'It's Primarily Around Their Viral Load': Public Health Decision-Making and HIV Risk Assemblages in Ontario, Canada.
items.summary: This study examined how public health practitioners in Ontario, Canada determine when the behaviors of people living with HIV constitute significant risk warranting formal intervention or enforcement orders. Using assemblage theory as a conceptual framework, the researchers conducted semi-structured interviews with public health personnel and analyzed the data through reflexive thematic analysis. They found that risk assessments were shaped by an interplay of biomedical markers such as viral load, care continuity, co-infection status, and compliance with directives, rather than by any single factor. The study also found that the Undetectable = Untransmittable principle has begun to reshape how risk is understood, effectively neutralizing some punitive responses, but its adoption was uneven across public health units, producing inconsistent and sometimes coercive outcomes. The authors argue that conceptualizing HIV risk as relationally produced rather than individually located challenges punitive governance approaches, and they call for broader equity-based adoption of current biomedical evidence to ensure nonpunitive public health responses.
items.citation: Daroya E, French M, Hastings C et al.. 'It's Primarily Around Their Viral Load': Public Health Decision-Making and HIV Risk Assemblages in Ontario, Canada.. Sociology of health & illness. 2026

items.title: Durable viral suppression in the dolutegravir era: a data-enabled, risk-stratified model for integrated HIV care in Uganda.
items.summary: This paper examined persistent gaps in HIV viral suppression and disease management in Uganda despite near-universal adoption of dolutegravir-based antiretroviral therapy, recognizing that treatment potency alone does not resolve service delivery failures. The authors analyzed a large routine-care cohort from a major Ugandan HIV organization alongside a regional systematic review and meta-analysis to characterize the burden and predictors of viral non-suppression, advanced HIV disease, and co-occurring tuberculosis and non-communicable diseases. They found that viral non-suppression remained measurable in the cohort and was substantially higher in the broader regional estimate, with failure clustering in identifiable social and clinical risk groups. In response, the authors propose a pragmatic, data-driven, risk-stratified care model that uses routine electronic health record signals to trigger timely viral load follow-up, deliver targeted adherence and socioeconomic support bundles to high-risk individuals, and integrate tuberculosis and non-communicable disease management within HIV platforms. The model centers on a focused accountability dashboard to translate antiretroviral scale-up into durable viral suppression and progress toward global HIV targets.
items.citation: Namaganda MM, Nabende JN, Kateete DP et al.. Durable viral suppression in the dolutegravir era: a data-enabled, risk-stratified model for integrated HIV care in Uganda.. Frontiers in public health. 2026

items.title: Optimizing hepatitis B diagnosis for mothers in a low-resource setting: A field pilot of Xpert point-of-care viral load testing in Ugandan antenatal clinics.
items.summary: This study examined whether point-of-care hepatitis B viral load testing using the Xpert HBV DNA assay could be feasibly and cost-effectively implemented in antenatal care settings in Uganda to support prevention of mother-to-child transmission. Conducted across ten high-volume hospitals in a two-phase design, the research first verified the assay's diagnostic accuracy against a standard centralized platform and then assessed its operational integration into routine antenatal care, including feasibility, usability, and cost. The Xpert assay showed full concordance with the reference platform, and the large majority of samples were successfully processed, with most results returned to providers and a substantial proportion delivered to mothers on the same day as testing. The cost per test was comparable to centralized testing while offering meaningful advantages in turnaround time and reduced patient visits, though challenges such as supply stockouts and competing laboratory priorities were noted. The authors conclude that the Xpert platform represents a timely, accurate, and operationally viable approach to expanding hepatitis B prophylaxis and treatment for pregnant women in resource-limited settings.
items.citation: Nabitaka LK, Kasone V, Olal E et al.. Optimizing hepatitis B diagnosis for mothers in a low-resource setting: A field pilot of Xpert point-of-care viral load testing in Ugandan antenatal clinics.. PLOS global public health. 2026

items.title: Comparative evaluation of a multi-antigen malaria rapid diagnostic test against qPCR and microscopy in a co-endemic area of Southern Ethiopia.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a multi-antigen rapid diagnostic test for malaria in a region of southern Ethiopia where both *Plasmodium falciparum* and *P. vivax* co-circulate, conditions that challenge conventional HRP2-based tests due to low parasite densities and gene deletions. Febrile patients presenting at health facilities during peak transmission season were tested using the new Bioline multi-antigen RDT alongside conventional RDTs, expert microscopy, and quantitative PCR. The multi-antigen RDT showed high specificity and strong agreement with expert microscopy, but its sensitivity dropped notably when qPCR served as the reference standard, revealing that submicroscopic, low-density infections were frequently missed. Molecular testing detected the highest overall malaria prevalence and identified more cases of each species and mixed infections than either microscopy or the RDT. The authors conclude that while the multi-antigen RDT is clinically useful for routine diagnosis, more sensitive tools are needed for surveillance and elimination efforts in endemic settings.
items.citation: Bogale A, Mitiku A, Tefera S et al.. Comparative evaluation of a multi-antigen malaria rapid diagnostic test against qPCR and microscopy in a co-endemic area of Southern Ethiopia.. Scientific reports. 2026

items.title: Characterization of arbovirus infections in patients within Haiti by screening discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes.
items.summary: This study investigated circulating arboviruses in Haiti during a period when security concerns had disrupted conventional surveillance efforts. Researchers extracted nucleic acids from discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes collected from febrile patients at healthcare centers across two Haitian departments between 2021 and 2023, then screened these samples using real-time PCR assays targeting flaviviruses, alphaviruses, and Oropouche virus, followed by next-generation sequencing and phylogenetic analysis. Dengue virus serotype 3 was identified in a substantial number of samples, all collected in 2023, while no alphaviruses or Oropouche virus were detected. Phylogenetic analysis showed that the identified sequences were closely related to strains circulating in the Caribbean region during 2022–2023, consistent with the broader dengue serotype 3 outbreak observed across the Americas in 2023. The findings demonstrate that discarded malaria rapid diagnostic tests can serve as a practical and valuable surveillance tool for detecting and characterizing arboviruses in resource-limited or politically unstable settings.
items.citation: Matulis GA, Smith HP, Hall G et al.. Characterization of arbovirus infections in patients within Haiti by screening discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes.. PLoS neglected tropical diseases. 2026

items.title: Scalable Visual Pretraining for Language Intelligence
items.summary: This paper challenges the assumption that large language models must be pretrained exclusively on text, arguing that converting visually rich documents into plain text discards meaningful information encoded in figures, equations, and page layouts. The authors conducted a systematic study of unsupervised visual pretraining paradigms that operate directly on visual documents without extracting text, evaluating multiple model backbones across a range of benchmarks. They found that visual pretraining on the same underlying corpora consistently outperforms text-only pretraining, suggesting that treating documents as visual inputs rather than text sequences provides a more informative and scalable training signal for foundation model intelligence.
items.citation: Yiming Zhang, Zhonghan Zhao, Wenwei Zhang et al.. Scalable Visual Pretraining for Language Intelligence. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models
items.summary: This paper examined how vision-language models have improved at describing complex social scenes over roughly a decade of development. The researchers introduced a new dataset of images depicting complex social interactions and behaviors, then evaluated nine models spanning pre-multimodal large language models through current multimodal large language models, comparing their performance against human-generated descriptions using a gold standard benchmark. They assessed five categories of visual-cognitive errors, including object detection, recognition, hallucination, scene understanding, and spatial dependence. The study found that earlier models performed far below even the lowest-ranked human descriptions, while current multimodal large language models now match the accuracy of top human describers and have effectively closed the performance gap between simple and complex scenes. Most error types have been nearly eliminated by modern models, with the exception of spatial dependence errors, and detection, recognition, and hallucination errors were found to have the greatest overall impact on description accuracy.
items.citation: Shravan Murlidaran, Miguel P. Eckstein. Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models. arXiv. 2026

items.title: VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents
items.summary: This paper investigated whether large language model agents could autonomously discover and exploit vulnerabilities in Internet of Things systems, an area that had not previously been explored despite growing interest in AI-driven penetration testing. The researchers developed a multi-agent framework called VEXAIoT, which pairs a vulnerability detection agent with an attack execution agent to conduct reconnaissance, plan attack sequences, and carry out exploits against vulnerable IoT services using LLM-based reasoning combined with offensive security tools. The system was evaluated across ten attack scenarios drawn from OWASP IoT vulnerability categories within two established testing environments, IoTGoat and Metasploitable, with experiments totaling hundreds of attack executions. The framework achieved a high overall success rate with low token overhead and fast execution times, demonstrating that LLM-driven multi-agent systems can effectively automate IoT vulnerability assessment in controlled settings. The authors conclude that this approach holds significant promise for scaling adaptive security testing to address the unique constraints and weaknesses characteristic of IoT deployments.
items.citation: Katherine Swinea, Kshitiz Aryal, Lopamudra Praharaj et al.. VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Spatial decomposition of Little Red Dots with JWST/NIRSpec IFU into broad-line red cores and narrow-line blue host galaxies
items.summary: This paper investigates Little Red Dots, a class of compact red objects discovered by JWST that display complex spectral features including V-shaped continua, broad Balmer emission lines, and in some cases Balmer absorption, whose physical origins remain contested. To test whether these features arise from a central engine plus host galaxy system, the researchers used JWST/NIRSpec integral field unit spectroscopy to spatially map different spectral components across a sample of broad Hα-selected Little Red Dots at redshift approximately five. By decomposing the continuum, narrow and broad emission lines, and absorption features spatially, they found that blue continuum emission is co-spatial with the narrow emission line region, while the red continuum originates from a compact core that coincides with the broad Balmer emission and absorption. Spatial mapping of the oxygen equivalent width further revealed a marked decrease at this central core.
