These results provide strong observational support for a two-component model in which a red, compact central engine—likely hosting a rapidly growing black hole—is embedded within a more extended blue host galaxy.
items.citation: Yuzo Ishikawa, Anna-Christina Eilers, Rohan P. Naidu et al.. Spatial decomposition of Little Red Dots with JWST/NIRSpec IFU into broad-line red cores and narrow-line blue host galaxies. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Lean-QIT: Towards a Formal Infrastructure for Quantum Information Theory
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of formalizing quantum information theory within a machine-checked proof system, specifically targeting the gap between rigorous mathematical proofs and computer-verified foundations. The authors developed Lean-QIT, a library built in Lean 4 that provides composable, kernel-verified interfaces for quantum states and channels, coding constructs, performance criteria, hypothesis testing, and asymptotic rate analysis. A key design choice was separating operational definitions of codes and error criteria from their information-theoretic characterizations, enabling reusable components for achievability and converse arguments. Using this infrastructure, the authors formally verified several landmark results in quantum information theory, including Schumacher's source-coding theorem, the Holevo–Schumacher–Westmoreland classical-capacity theorem, and the entanglement-assisted classical-capacity theorem along with its strong converse. The library is presented as a foundational substrate intended to support future AI-assisted formalization and automated proof search in quantum information and computation.
items.citation: Chengkai Zhu, Ziao Tang, Guocheng Zhen et al.. Lean-QIT: Towards a Formal Infrastructure for Quantum Information Theory. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Toward Real-Time Sentence-Level Sign Language Translation
items.summary: This paper investigates sentence-level sign language translation with an emphasis on real-time deployment rather than architectural novelty. The researchers fine-tuned a SHuBERT-ByT5 translation stack on a subset of the How2Sign dataset using QLoRA, keeping the sign language encoder frozen to manage computational constraints. The resulting model achieved competitive translation quality as measured by BLEU and BLEURT scores on held-out test data. Beyond the translation model, the primary contribution is a hardware-aware streaming pipeline that distributes camera capture and display to a low-power client device while offloading compute-intensive perception and translation to a backend server, making the system client-agnostic across devices. Several engineering optimizations—including chunked ingestion, parallelized perception, and a sentence-boundary state machine—substantially reduced both mean and tail response latency, demonstrating a viable path toward practical, real-time sign language translation.
items.citation: Thanh-Hoang Nguyen Doan. Toward Real-Time Sentence-Level Sign Language Translation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Emerging blood-based diagnostic strategies for tuberculosis.
items.summary: This review examines blood-based diagnostic approaches for tuberculosis, a disease caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis that continues to pose a major global public health burden. The authors focus on serological methods, which have gained renewed interest due to the ongoing identification of novel biomarkers and their practical advantages of simplicity, rapid turnaround, and cost-effectiveness. The review is particularly concerned with improving diagnosis in challenging clinical scenarios, including extrapulmonary tuberculosis and cases where conventional sputum culture and rapid molecular assays yield negative results. The authors survey current biomarkers employed in TB serodiagnosis, discuss how advanced diagnostic technologies can be integrated into these approaches, and assess their collective potential to enhance diagnostic accuracy across varied clinical settings.
items.citation: Yue C, Geng X, Sun X et al.. Emerging blood-based diagnostic strategies for tuberculosis.. Clinical chemistry and laboratory medicine. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic accuracy, operational feasibility, and cost considerations of Truenat MTB Plus and Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra for pulmonary tuberculosis.
items.summary: This study compared two WHO-endorsed nucleic acid amplification tests, Truenat MTB Plus and Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra, for diagnosing pulmonary tuberculosis in India, evaluating diagnostic accuracy alongside operational feasibility and cost. Using mycobacterial culture as the reference standard, a prospective study enrolled presumptive pulmonary tuberculosis patients at a tuberculosis centre in New Delhi, with paired sputum specimens tested on both platforms. Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra demonstrated higher sensitivity and specificity for detecting Mycobacterium tuberculosis and produced far fewer invalid and indeterminate results compared to Truenat MTB Plus, which had notably higher rates of both, increasing the need for repeat testing. However, Truenat was substantially less expensive per test, making it more attractive for decentralized, resource-limited settings. The authors conclude that the two platforms are complementary, and advocate a tiered diagnostic approach that leverages each platform's strengths to broaden access to molecular tuberculosis testing.
items.citation: Sidiq Z, Anand A, Dwivedi KK et al.. Diagnostic accuracy, operational feasibility, and cost considerations of Truenat MTB Plus and Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra for pulmonary tuberculosis.. PloS one. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic Accuracy of Molecular Testing on Saliva and Oral Swabs for Pulmonary Tuberculosis.
items.summary: This study evaluated whether molecular testing on saliva and oral swabs could serve as accurate, nonsputum alternatives for diagnosing pulmonary tuberculosis. Using a nested case-control design within a prospective cohort of adults and children at primary care centers in Colombia, researchers collected paired saliva and nylon-flocked oral swab samples from participants undergoing tuberculosis evaluation, testing them with Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra against a sputum liquid mycobacterial culture reference standard. Saliva demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity for culture-confirmed tuberculosis, surpassing the World Health Organization's target threshold for nonsputum diagnostic tests, while oral swabs showed lower sensitivity despite similarly high specificity. Saliva sensitivity was significantly greater than that of swabs, and both collection methods were well accepted by participants. The findings support saliva-based molecular testing as a promising nonsputum approach to closing gaps in tuberculosis detection.
items.venue: Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America
items.citation: Vargas DA, Fuertes-Bucheli JF, Sanchez-Hidalgo A et al.. Diagnostic Accuracy of Molecular Testing on Saliva and Oral Swabs for Pulmonary Tuberculosis.. Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America. 2026

items.title: Comparative Diagnostic Accuracy of GeneXpert MTB/RIF and TB-LAMP for Tuberculosis Detection Among Presumptive Tuberculosis Cases in Bayelsa State, Nigeria
items.summary: This study evaluated and compared the diagnostic accuracy of two rapid nucleic acid amplification tests, GeneXpert MTB/RIF and TB-LAMP, for detecting tuberculosis among presumptive cases in a high-burden Nigerian setting, using mycobacterial culture on Löwenstein–Jensen medium as the reference standard. A cross-sectional design was employed across multiple health facilities in Bayelsa State, with sputum samples from several hundred participants tested in parallel by all three methods. Both rapid tests demonstrated high sensitivity and negative predictive value, but GeneXpert showed stronger agreement with culture and superior specificity, while TB-LAMP detected a higher proportion of positive cases, partly attributable to false-positive results from non-tuberculous mycobacteria. GeneXpert's additional capacity to identify rifampicin resistance supports its role as a confirmatory tool, particularly where drug-resistant tuberculosis is suspected. The authors propose a sequential algorithm in which TB-LAMP serves as an initial low-cost screening step at peripheral facilities, followed by GeneXpert confirmation, as a strategy to improve case detection while making efficient use of limited resources.
items.citation: UMUWE P, Wariso KT, ILOZUMBA J et al.. Comparative Diagnostic Accuracy of GeneXpert MTB/RIF and TB-LAMP for Tuberculosis Detection Among Presumptive Tuberculosis Cases in Bayelsa State, Nigeria. 2026

items.title: Field-ready portable rapid nucleic acid test for tuberculosis detection and drug-resistance profiling in resource-limited settings
items.summary: This paper developed a portable, field-deployable diagnostic platform for tuberculosis that integrates nucleic acid amplification with artificial intelligence-assisted readout in a single unified system. The platform uses loop-mediated isothermal amplification targeting dual Mycobacterium tuberculosis genetic markers, paired with a compact device and smartphone-based results interpretation, enabling both primary TB detection and drug-resistance profiling without any alteration to the device's physical configuration. Clinical evaluation across a set of patient samples demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity, and real-world deployment in a primary healthcare setting by minimally trained personnel achieved sensitivity and perfect specificity comparable to the established GeneXpert benchmark. Beyond screening, the same platform architecture was extended to identify mutations associated with resistance to rifampicin and isoniazid through a probe-free, allele-specific amplification approach. The authors conclude that combining robust molecular diagnostics with AI-driven automation in an accessible format represents a meaningful step toward decentralizing tuberculosis diagnosis in high-burden, resource-limited environments.
items.citation: Nag S, Banerjee S, Banerjee S et al.. Field-ready portable rapid nucleic acid test for tuberculosis detection and drug-resistance profiling in resource-limited settings. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic accuracy of GeneReady MTB assay on sputum for detecting pulmonary tuberculosis: a prospective multicenter study in China.
items.summary: This prospective multicenter study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a novel molecular test, the GeneReady MTB assay, for detecting pulmonary tuberculosis in sputum samples from patients with suggestive symptoms across three Chinese hospitals. Participants were tested simultaneously with GeneReady, smear microscopy, mycobacterial culture, and the established Xpert MTB/RIF assay, allowing direct head-to-head comparison. Against a microbiological reference standard, GeneReady demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity, with performance stratified by bacterial load showing somewhat lower sensitivity in smear-negative compared to smear-positive cases. Agreement between GeneReady and the Xpert MTB/RIF assay was strong for both positive and negative results. The authors conclude that GeneReady offers diagnostic accuracy comparable to Xpert MTB/RIF while providing advantages of lower cost, faster turnaround, and simpler operation, supporting its potential utility as an initial diagnostic tool for pulmonary tuberculosis in resource-limited settings.
items.citation: Shang Y, Tang Z, Deng Y et al.. Diagnostic accuracy of GeneReady MTB assay on sputum for detecting pulmonary tuberculosis: a prospective multicenter study in China.. Journal of clinical microbiology. 2026

items.title: Scalable Visual Pretraining for Language Intelligence
items.summary: This paper challenges the prevailing assumption that large language models should be pretrained exclusively on text, arguing that visually rich documents contain information in figures, equations, and layouts that text extraction fails to capture. The authors conducted a systematic study of unsupervised visual pretraining paradigms that train directly on visual documents rather than converting them to plain text. Testing across multiple model backbones and benchmarks, they compared visual pretraining against text-only pretraining on equivalent underlying corpora. Their findings consistently show that visual pretraining outperforms text-only pretraining, suggesting it represents a scalable and efficient approach to building foundation model intelligence that better exploits the full informational content of documents and web pages.
items.citation: Yiming Zhang, Zhonghan Zhao, Wenwei Zhang et al.. Scalable Visual Pretraining for Language Intelligence. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models
items.summary: This paper examined how vision-language models have progressed over a decade in their ability to describe complex social scenes, an area underexplored by standard benchmarks. The researchers introduced a new dataset of images depicting complex social interactions and behaviors, then evaluated nine models spanning from early pre-multimodal systems to recent large language model-based approaches alongside human descriptions, assessing performance against a gold standard. They tracked five categories of visual-cognitive errors, including object detection, recognition, hallucination, scene understanding, and spatial dependence. Results showed that earlier models performed far worse than even the lowest-ranked human describers, while recent multimodal large language models reached accuracy levels comparable to top human performers and effectively closed the performance gap between simple and complex scenes. Most error types were nearly eliminated by modern models, though spatial dependence errors persisted, and detection, recognition, and hallucination errors were identified as having the greatest negative impact on description accuracy overall.
items.citation: Shravan Murlidaran, Miguel P. Eckstein. Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models. arXiv. 2026

items.title: VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents
items.summary: This paper investigated whether large language model agents could autonomously discover and exploit vulnerabilities in Internet of Things systems, an area previously unexplored despite growing interest in AI-driven penetration testing. The researchers developed a multi-agent framework called VEXAIoT, combining a vulnerability detection agent and an attack execution agent that together perform reconnaissance, plan attack sequences, and execute exploits against IoT services using LLM-based reasoning paired with offensive security tools. The framework was evaluated across ten attack scenarios mapped to established OWASP IoT vulnerability categories within two controlled testbed environments, IoTGoat and Metasploitable. Results showed a very high overall attack success rate across hundreds of executions, with most attacks completing in under two minutes and requiring low computational token overhead. The authors conclude that LLM-driven multi-agent systems can effectively automate IoT vulnerability assessment and offensive security workflows, demonstrating both the promise and the implied risks of applying such technology to IoT security testing.
items.citation: Katherine Swinea, Kshitiz Aryal, Lopamudra Praharaj et al.. VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Spatial decomposition of Little Red Dots with JWST/NIRSpec IFU into broad-line red cores and narrow-line blue host galaxies
items.summary: This paper investigates Little Red Dots, a population of compact red sources discovered by JWST whose unusual spectral properties — including a V-shaped continuum, broad Balmer emission lines, and Balmer absorption — have debated physical origins. The researchers used JWST/NIRSpec integral field unit spectroscopy to spatially resolve and decompose the spectral components of several broad Hα-selected Little Red Dots at high redshift, employing both prism and high-resolution grating observations. By mapping the spatial distribution of the blue and red continuum, narrow and broad emission lines, and absorption features, they found that the blue continuum is co-spatial with the narrow emission line region while the red continuum originates from a compact core coincident with the broad Balmer emission and absorption. The central core also shows a marked decrease in oxygen equivalent width relative to the surrounding region. These findings support a two-component physical model in which a red, compact central engine — likely hosting a rapidly accreting black hole — is embedded within a more extended blue host galaxy.
items.citation: Yuzo Ishikawa, Anna-Christina Eilers, Rohan P. Naidu et al.. Spatial decomposition of Little Red Dots with JWST/NIRSpec IFU into broad-line red cores and narrow-line blue host galaxies. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Lean-QIT: Towards a Formal Infrastructure for Quantum Information Theory
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of formally verifying quantum information theory within a machine-checked proof system, noting that existing formal developments lack a reusable operational layer that cleanly separates coding protocols from their information-theoretic characterizations. The authors developed Lean-QIT, a library built in Lean 4 that provides composable, kernel-verified interfaces for quantum states and channels, source and channel codes, finite-block performance criteria, hypothesis testing, one-shot quantities, and asymptotic rate constructions. Using this infrastructure, they formally proved several landmark results: Schumacher's quantum source-coding theorem, the Holevo–Schumacher–Westmoreland theorem for classical capacity of quantum channels, and the entanglement-assisted classical-capacity theorem along with its strong converse. The library is designed with modularity in mind, separating operational definitions from analytic characterizations and exposing reusable achievability, converse, and asymptotic components that can be combined across different theorems. The authors position Lean-QIT as both a foundational machine-readable substrate for formal quantum information theory and a platform suited for AI-assisted formalization and automated proof search in quantum information and computation.
items.citation: Chengkai Zhu, Ziao Tang, Guocheng Zhen et al.. Lean-QIT: Towards a Formal Infrastructure for Quantum Information Theory. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Toward Real-Time Sentence-Level Sign Language Translation
items.summary: This paper investigates sentence-level sign language translation with an explicit focus on achieving real-time performance for practical deployment rather than advancing translation architectures. The researchers fine-tuned a SHuBERT-ByT5 translation stack on a subset of the How2Sign dataset using QLoRA, keeping the SHuBERT component frozen to manage computational constraints. The system achieved competitive translation quality as measured by BLEU and BLEURT scores on validation and test splits. The central contribution is a hardware-aware streaming architecture in which a low-cost single-board computer handles camera input and output while offloading perception and translation to a more powerful backend, with the client-agnostic design allowing the same backend to serve multiple device types. A suite of engineering optimizations—including chunked ingestion, parallelized perception, bounded queues, temporal reordering, and a sentence-boundary state machine—substantially reduced both mean and high-percentile response latency compared to a baseline system.
items.citation: Thanh-Hoang Nguyen Doan. Toward Real-Time Sentence-Level Sign Language Translation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Point-of-care screening for curable sexually transmitted infections in antenatal care in Zimbabwe: a costing study.
items.summary: This study examined the costs of integrating point-of-care screening for three curable sexually transmitted infections into antenatal care in Zimbabwe, where such infections are linked to adverse birth outcomes but cost data have been scarce. Researchers implemented screening at two primary healthcare clinics in Harare and collected economic costs from a health system perspective using staff interviews, time-and-motion studies, and other data sources, then modelled implementation in a low-volume routine care setting over a twelve-month period. The costs were compared against a modelled standard of care based on syndromic management, and incremental costs per person screened and treated were calculated. Test kits and personnel together accounted for the large majority of total intervention costs, and the incremental cost per person treated was substantially higher than syndromic management. The authors conclude that reducing the price of point-of-care diagnostics will be essential for making antenatal STI screening affordable in this context.
items.citation: Martin K, Bath D, Nyamwanza R et al.. Point-of-care screening for curable sexually transmitted infections in antenatal care in Zimbabwe: a costing study.. Sexually transmitted infections. 2026

items.title: Cost-effectiveness of a decentralised molecular point-of-care testing programme for sexually transmitted infections in remote primary care health services in Australia.
items.summary: This study examined the cost-effectiveness of a decentralised molecular point-of-care testing programme for sexually transmitted infections in remote Australian First Nations communities, where the burden of chlamydia, gonorrhoea, and trichomonas is disproportionately high. Using a decision analytic model with a government payer perspective and a ten-year time horizon, researchers compared the costs and health outcomes of point-of-care testing against standard laboratory-based testing for young women and men aged sixteen to twenty-nine years. The primary outcome measure was cost per quality-adjusted life year gained, with additional outcomes including rates of pelvic inflammatory disease and adverse birth outcomes. The analysis found that the point-of-care programme was cost-effective relative to the accepted willingness-to-pay threshold, and was predicted to substantially reduce diagnosed pelvic inflammatory disease and preterm or low birth weight births among those with infections. The results were most sensitive to the probability of infection and the likelihood of receiving treatment within two days, supporting the conclusion that sustained point-of-care testing in remote primary care settings is likely to improve reproductive health outcomes.
items.citation: Watts CG, Causer LM, Matthews SJ et al.. Cost-effectiveness of a decentralised molecular point-of-care testing programme for sexually transmitted infections in remote primary care health services in Australia.. Sexually transmitted infections. 2026

items.title: Use of point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in community outreach settings in Dallas, Texas, USA.
items.summary: This study examined the implementation of rapid point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in non-clinical, community outreach settings in an urban environment. Researchers conducted a program evaluation and retrospective data review of individuals tested at these sites, using statistical tests to explore whether demographic characteristics or symptom presence were associated with testing positive. The testing platform delivered results within thirty minutes, and all individuals who tested positive for either infection were referred for treatment within one day. No statistically significant associations were found between test positivity and demographics or symptoms, though the analysis was constrained by a small sample size. The authors conclude that point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing can enable rapid diagnosis and same-day treatment referral in outreach settings, while calling for further research into scalability, cost, and treatment completion.
items.citation: Blanchard HL, Gonzalez E, Chow J. Use of point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in community outreach settings in Dallas, Texas, USA.. Sexual health. 2026

items.title: From syndromic management to diagnostic-led management: a report from an expert meeting on introducing sexually transmitted infection testing in low-resource settings.
items.summary: This paper examined the challenges and opportunities involved in transitioning from syndromic management to diagnostic-led management of sexually transmitted infections in low-resource settings, with a focus on sub-Saharan Africa. An expert meeting convened in Nairobi brought together dozens of specialists across STI diagnostics, policy, regulation, and clinical practice to identify priorities and strategies for scaling up point-of-care testing. Participants analyzed barriers and opportunities at four levels — individual client, health service delivery, national, and global — and generated recommendations at each level. Key recommendations included improving public knowledge of STIs, optimizing clinical workflows, expanding testing beyond traditional health facilities, updating national treatment guidelines, strengthening laboratory quality assurance, and harmonizing international regulatory requirements. The authors conclude that coordinated multi-level efforts are needed to ensure equitable introduction of point-of-care STI diagnostics, which they argue will improve care quality, curb antimicrobial resistance, and strengthen STI control overall.
items.citation: Manguro GO, Ray J, Schocken C et al.. From syndromic management to diagnostic-led management: a report from an expert meeting on introducing sexually transmitted infection testing in low-resource settings.. Sexually transmitted infections. 2026

items.title: Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models
items.summary: This paper examined how vision-language models have progressed in describing complex visual scenes over roughly a decade, focusing on error types that previous evaluations have largely overlooked. The researchers introduced a new dataset of images depicting complex social interactions and behaviors, which they used alongside an established simpler benchmark to assess model performance from early systems through the latest multimodal large language models. They evaluated scene description accuracy against a gold standard and categorized errors into five visual-cognitive types, including object detection, hallucination, and spatial dependence. The findings show that earlier models fell well below human-level performance on complex social scenes, while the most recent multimodal large language models achieved accuracies comparable to top-performing human descriptions and effectively closed the gap between simple and complex scene understanding. Nearly all error types were eliminated by the latest models, with the notable exception of spatial dependence errors, and detection, recognition, and hallucination errors were identified as having the greatest impact on overall accuracy.
items.citation: Shravan Murlidaran, Miguel P. Eckstein. Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models. arXiv. 2026

items.title: VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents
items.summary: This paper investigated whether large language model agents could autonomously discover and exploit vulnerabilities in Internet of Things systems, a domain that had not previously been explored with this approach. The researchers developed a multi-agent framework called VEXAIoT, comprising a vulnerability detection agent and an attack execution agent that together perform reconnaissance, plan attack sequences, and execute exploits against IoT services using LLM-based reasoning combined with offensive security tools. The framework was evaluated across ten attack scenarios mapped to OWASP IoT vulnerability categories within IoTGoat and Metasploitable environments. Results showed a very high overall attack success rate across hundreds of executions, with most attacks completing in under two minutes and low computational token overhead. The authors conclude that LLM-driven multi-agent systems show strong potential for automating IoT vulnerability assessment and offensive security workflows in controlled testing environments.
items.citation: Katherine Swinea, Kshitiz Aryal, Lopamudra Praharaj et al.. VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Spatial decomposition of Little Red Dots with JWST/NIRSpec IFU into broad-line red cores and narrow-line blue host galaxies
items.summary: This paper investigates the physical nature of Little Red Dots, a population of compact red sources discovered by JWST that display unusual spectral features including a V-shaped continuum, broad Balmer emission lines, and in some cases Balmer absorption. To test whether these features arise from two distinct components — a central engine hosting a rapidly growing black hole plus a surrounding host galaxy — the researchers used JWST/NIRSpec integral field unit spectroscopy to spatially resolve and map the continuum, emission lines, and absorption across a sample of broad Hα-selected Little Red Dots at redshift approximately five. By analyzing both prism and high-resolution grating observations, they found that the blue continuum emission is spatially coincident with the narrow emission line region, while the red continuum is concentrated in a compact core co-spatial with the broad Balmer emission and absorption. Spatial mapping of the oxygen emission line equivalent width further revealed a pronounced suppression in the central core, consistent with AGN continuum dilution. Taken together, these results provide strong observational evidence that Little Red Dots are composed of at least two physically distinct components: a red, compact central engine embedded within a blue, more extended host galaxy.
items.citation: Yuzo Ishikawa, Anna-Christina Eilers, Rohan P. Naidu et al.. Spatial decomposition of Little Red Dots with JWST/NIRSpec IFU into broad-line red cores and narrow-line blue host galaxies. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Lean-QIT: Towards a Formal Infrastructure for Quantum Information Theory
items.summary: This paper introduces Lean-QIT, a formal library built in the Lean 4 proof assistant designed to provide a rigorous, machine-checked infrastructure for quantum information theory. The authors identified a gap in existing formalization efforts: the absence of a reusable operational layer that cleanly separates the definition of codes, error criteria, achievable rates, and channel capacities from their information-theoretic characterizations. To address this, they constructed composable interfaces covering quantum states and channels, source and channel coding, hypothesis testing, one-shot quantities, and asymptotic rate constructions. Using this framework, they formally verified several foundational coding theorems, including Schumacher's quantum source-coding theorem, the Holevo–Schumacher–Westmoreland classical-capacity theorem, and the entanglement-assisted classical-capacity theorem along with its strong converse. The resulting library is positioned both as a machine-readable foundation for further formal work in quantum information theory and as a compositional substrate for AI-assisted formalization and automated proof search.
items.citation: Chengkai Zhu, Ziao Tang, Guocheng Zhen et al.. Lean-QIT: Towards a Formal Infrastructure for Quantum Information Theory. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Toward Real-Time Sentence-Level Sign Language Translation
items.summary: This paper investigates sentence-level sign language translation with an explicit focus on real-time deployment rather than architectural novelty. The researchers fine-tuned a SHuBERT-ByT5 translation stack on a subset of the How2Sign dataset using QLoRA with the SHuBERT encoder kept frozen, working within practical compute and storage constraints. The system achieved moderate translation quality on the test split as measured by BLEU and BLEURT scores. A central contribution is a hardware-aware streaming architecture in which a Raspberry Pi client handles camera capture and output while perception and translation are offloaded to a CPU/GPU backend, making the system client-agnostic across devices including browsers and phones. Through engineering optimizations such as chunked ingestion, parallelized perception, bounded queues, and a sentence-boundary state machine, the system substantially reduced both mean and ninety-fifth percentile post-finalization response latency compared to a baseline pipeline.
items.citation: Thanh-Hoang Nguyen Doan. Toward Real-Time Sentence-Level Sign Language Translation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Symbol-Level Precoding for Continuous-Aperture ISAC Systems
items.summary: This paper examines integrated sensing and communication systems that use continuous-aperture arrays, which provide rich electromagnetic degrees of freedom but present mathematically challenging infinite-dimensional optimization problems. The authors propose a symbol-level precoding framework for a downlink system in which the transmit current distribution is optimized to maximize power directed at sensing targets while simultaneously satisfying constructive-interference constraints that guarantee reliable communication. A key theoretical contribution is the proof that the optimal current distribution resides in a finite-dimensional subspace defined by the electromagnetic responses associated with both communication and sensing functions, enabling an exact and structure-preserving reformulation of the originally intractable problem. Building on this result, the authors develop a penalty projected-gradient algorithm to jointly optimize the finite-dimensional current coefficients alongside receive polarization combiners. Simulations show that the proposed approach outperforms both conventional Fourier-basis continuous-aperture and spatially discrete array benchmarks in terms of sensing utility and communication reliability.
items.citation: Hongli Liu, Qiang Li. Symbol-Level Precoding for Continuous-Aperture ISAC Systems. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Association between menstrual-related disorders and sexually transmitted infections: A nationwide cross-sectional study in Japan.
items.summary: This nationwide cross-sectional study investigated whether menstrual-related disorders are associated with sexually transmitted infections among young women in Japan, and whether this relationship differs by disorder type or hormonal therapy use. Using a large insurance claims database, researchers identified women under forty with diagnoses of endometriosis or dysmenorrhea and compared the prevalence of five sexually transmitted infections against women without these conditions. Women with menstrual-related disorders showed substantially higher prevalence of all examined infections, with endometriosis showing the strongest association, particularly for genital chlamydia infection. Hormonal therapy use had only minimal influence on the observed differences in infection prevalence within the affected group. The authors conclude that gynecologic care for women with endometriosis or dysmenorrhea should incorporate sexually transmitted infection screening, while noting that increased healthcare-seeking behavior and diagnostic patterns may partly explain the observed associations.
items.citation: Yoshihara T, Owada S, Arita H et al.. Association between menstrual-related disorders and sexually transmitted infections: A nationwide cross-sectional study in Japan.. PloS one. 2026

query: CRISPR based diagnostics infectious disease detection

items.title: CRISPR-Cas and Infectious Diseases: A Decade of Translational Advances in Molecular Biotechnology.
items.summary: This review examines the translational progress of CRISPR-Cas systems across three major applications in infectious disease management: diagnostics, therapeutic genome editing, and vaccine development. The authors surveyed developments over the past decade, highlighting diagnostic platforms capable of rapid and sensitive pathogen detection as well as CRISPR-based strategies for targeting viral genomes, combating antimicrobial resistance, and engineering both live-attenuated and personalized neoantigen vaccines. They find that CRISPR-based diagnostics have advanced furthest toward clinical use, having moved from proof-of-concept to preclinical and early clinical evaluation, while therapeutic and personalized vaccine applications remain at more exploratory stages owing to challenges in delivery and safety. The review critically assesses technical, regulatory, and ethical barriers across all three domains within a unified infectious disease framework. The authors conclude that although CRISPR-Cas holds transformative potential, issues including delivery efficiency, off-target effects, and equitable regulatory oversight must be resolved before safe clinical translation can be achieved.
items.citation: Mayuri K, Saravanan KM, Somala CS et al.. CRISPR-Cas and Infectious Diseases: A Decade of Translational Advances in Molecular Biotechnology.. Biochemical genetics. 2026

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review paper examines the current landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the global need for decentralized, rapid, and accessible testing. The authors organized their analysis around the three core operational steps of such diagnostics: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection. For extraction, they evaluated approaches including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic methods, emphasizing designs that are simplified and equipment-free. Amplification strategies reviewed included both thermal cycling methods such as PCR and isothermal alternatives such as LAMP, RPA, and NASBA, all adapted for portable and low-power conditions. On the detection side, the review spans from colorimetric and fluorescent lateral flow assays to advanced biosensor platforms exploiting CRISPR-Cas systems and toehold switch mechanisms, which offer highly sensitive and programmable nucleic acid recognition.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

items.title: Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.
items.summary: This review examined the landscape of molecular diagnostic technologies for bacterial infections, with particular emphasis on their utility in addressing the challenges posed by antimicrobial resistance and the limitations of conventional culture-based methods. The authors conducted a structured comparative analysis of major platforms, including various forms of PCR, isothermal amplification, next-generation sequencing, CRISPR-based diagnostics, and digital PCR, evaluating each against key analytical parameters such as sensitivity, specificity, detection limits, turnaround time, and multiplexing capacity. The review also critically assessed the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning into diagnostic workflows for resistance prediction and clinical decision support, as well as the feasibility of deploying these technologies at the point of care. The authors found that each platform carries distinct strengths and limitations, and that translating these technologies into real-world clinical settings requires careful balancing of analytical performance against clinical interpretability and operational constraints. The review concludes by outlining key challenges and future directions for advancing culture-independent bacteriological diagnostics in both high- and resource-limited settings.
items.citation: Cotelici AM, Bălășoiu AT, Boldeanu MV et al.. Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.. International journal of molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: CRISPR/Cas12a2-Mediated Ultrasensitive Assay for Rapid Detection of H1N1 Influenza Virus RNA.
items.summary: This study developed a novel diagnostic platform using the CRISPR-Cas12a2 system to detect H1N1 influenza virus RNA with high sensitivity and speed. The assay targeted the conserved M gene of influenza A H1N1, combining optimized guide RNA design with the dual RNA cleavage and collateral single-stranded DNA degradation activities of SuCas12a2, paired with reverse transcription PCR and fluorescence-based readout. The platform achieved a very low detection limit within a short timeframe and demonstrated complete specificity against other respiratory pathogens in laboratory testing. Clinical validation using nasopharyngeal swab samples showed strong concordance with standard real-time quantitative PCR while also identifying additional positive cases that the reference method missed, suggesting superior sensitivity. The authors conclude that this CRISPR-Cas12a2-based approach represents a meaningful advance in molecular diagnostics for rapid and accurate detection of influenza during outbreak settings.
items.citation: Wen C, Min X, Yu H et al.. CRISPR/Cas12a2-Mediated Ultrasensitive Assay for Rapid Detection of H1N1 Influenza Virus RNA.. ACS omega. 2026

items.title: Synthetic Biology-Enabled Biosensing Platforms for Point-of-Care In Vitro Diagnostics: Programmable Modules, Clinical Applications, and Translational Challenges.
items.summary: This review examines how synthetic biology is transforming point-of-care in vitro diagnostics by enabling programmable, modular biosensing systems that overcome the limitations of conventional fixed-chemistry assays reliant on centralized laboratory infrastructure. The authors organize synthetic biology-based diagnostic platforms into four functional layers—molecular recognition, signal transduction and amplification, output generation, and system integration—and discuss four major enabling technologies: cell-free protein synthesis systems, aptamer and riboswitch sensors, CRISPR-Cas diagnostic platforms, and microfluidic integration. Clinical applications spanning infectious disease detection, cancer biomarker analysis, and drug metabolism and toxicity screening from recent years are surveyed alongside practical implementation considerations such as matrix tolerance, workflow complexity, manufacturability, and regulatory readiness. The review concludes by identifying future directions including artificial intelligence-assisted biosensor design, multimodal readout strategies, interoperable platform architectures, and the need for rigorous real-world clinical validation to facilitate translation of these systems into routine diagnostic use.
items.citation: Bao C, Zhang H, Jiang L et al.. Synthetic Biology-Enabled Biosensing Platforms for Point-of-Care In Vitro Diagnostics: Programmable Modules, Clinical Applications, and Translational Challenges.. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis
items.summary: This paper introduces a topological signal processing framework for analyzing EEG recordings associated with dream states, aiming to move beyond conventional spectral and statistical features. The authors apply sliding-window delay embeddings and Vietoris-Rips filtrations to multichannel pre-awakening EEG data to extract so-called Dynamic Betti Curves, which capture the geometric and topological structure of neural activity rather than its frequency-domain energy distribution. These topological invariants are integrated into a neural network architecture and combined with a topology-conditioned flow matching model capable of synthesizing dream-state EEG signals. The framework is analytically projected to substantially outperform existing power spectral density and catch22 benchmarks on a large multi-laboratory awakening database, though the authors explicitly note that the proposed Betti transition archetypes linking topology to dream phenomenology remain an exploratory hypothesis space awaiting empirical validation. The work positions phase-space geometry as a candidate paradigm shift in neural rare-event detection with potential applications in wearable brain-computer interface dream monitoring.
items.citation: Ren Takahashi, Emre Yusuf, Jayabrata Bhaduri. PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Impact of Benign Connectivity Variations on Intrusion Detection for Encrypted OPC UA Traffic in Industrial Private 5G Networks
items.summary: This paper investigated how machine learning-based intrusion detection systems behave when monitoring encrypted industrial communications in private 5G network environments, specifically focusing on OPC UA traffic. The researchers examined whether normal, benign connectivity variations in these networks could adversely affect the performance of such detection systems. Their experiments revealed that legitimate connectivity events can substantially increase false positive rates even in the complete absence of any actual attacks. Additionally, elevated anomaly scores from the intrusion detection system were found to frequently coincide with control-plane activity associated with these benign connectivity events. The authors conclude that control-plane context must be taken into account when interpreting intrusion detection outputs in industrial private 5G deployments to avoid mischaracterizing normal network behavior as malicious.
items.citation: Song Son Ha, Florian Foerster, Henry Beuster et al.. Impact of Benign Connectivity Variations on Intrusion Detection for Encrypted OPC UA Traffic in Industrial Private 5G Networks. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Identifying and characterizing extragalactic circum-CBC exoplanets with future gravitational-wave detectors
items.summary: This paper investigates whether exoplanets orbiting compact binary coalescences outside the Milky Way could be detected using gravitational wave observations, extending the known exoplanet census beyond the Galaxy for the first time. The authors exploit the fact that a companion planet causes the compact binary's centre of mass to wobble around the shared barycentre, imprinting a characteristic signature on the emitted gravitational waveform. They apply a technique that reconstructs the external gravitational potential by analysing the centre-of-mass kinematics encoded in the waveform, testing it against a range of circum-binary exoplanet configurations in both circular and eccentric orbits across next-generation detectors including an upgraded LIGO, Einstein Telescope, and DECIGO. For a substantial portion of the parameter space examined, the method can confirm the presence of an exoplanet and recover its mass to within an order-unity factor at moderate statistical confidence, with the main degeneracy being the unknown orbital inclination angle. The work serves as a proof of principle that future gravitational wave detectors could open an entirely new extragalactic channel for exoplanet discovery around some of the most extreme stellar remnants in the universe.
items.citation: Avinash Tiwari, Aditya Vijaykumar, Shasvath J. Kapadia et al.. Identifying and characterizing extragalactic circum-CBC exoplanets with future gravitational-wave detectors. arXiv. 2026

items.title: HD 148797: A bright F-type star with two moderate-period low-density sub-Jovian planets. Compact multi-planet architectures are common in the Neptunian savanna
items.summary: The paper reports the discovery and characterization of two transiting sub-Jovian planets orbiting the bright F-type star HD 148797, using photometric data from the TESS and CHEOPS space telescopes. The two planets have orbital periods near the golden mean ratio and similar sizes just above eight Earth radii; their masses were determined through photodynamical modeling of anti-correlated transit-timing variations rather than radial velocities, yielding bulk densities well below that of water and placing both firmly in the low-density Neptunian savanna population. The authors then examined the broader population of multi-planet systems in the Neptunian savanna and found that detected multi-system fractions are very high and that most such systems contain adjacent planet pairs with modest period ratios, suggesting these planets inhabit dynamically cold architectures. This statistical pattern is interpreted as evidence favoring smooth disk-driven migration over disruptive high-eccentricity tidal migration as the dominant pathway producing savanna sub-Jovians. The co-evolved, similarly sized nature of the two planets and the brightness of their host star also make this system an attractive candidate for comparative atmospheric studies.
items.citation: J. M. Almenara, A. Castro-González, R. Mardling et al.. HD 148797: A bright F-type star with two moderate-period low-density sub-Jovian planets. Compact multi-planet architectures are common in the Neptunian savanna. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models
items.summary: This paper examined how vision-language models have progressed in describing complex social scenes over roughly a decade, addressing limitations of prior evaluations that relied on simple imagery and coarse accuracy metrics. The researchers introduced a new dataset of images depicting complex social interactions and behaviors, then assessed nine models spanning pre-multimodal large language model and multimodal large language model eras alongside human descriptions, using a gold-standard benchmark and five defined visual-cognitive error categories. They found that earlier models performed substantially worse than even the lowest-ranked human descriptions on complex scenes, while recent multimodal large language models reached accuracy levels comparable to the best human descriptions. Notably, the newer models effectively closed the performance gap between simple and complex scenes and nearly eliminated most error types, though they continued to show occasional spatial dependence errors by emphasizing different image regions than humans do. The analysis also revealed that object detection, recognition, and hallucination errors carry the greatest weight in determining overall scene description accuracy.
items.citation: Shravan Murlidaran, Miguel P. Eckstein. Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models. arXiv. 2026

items.title: VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents
items.summary: This paper investigated whether large language model agents could autonomously discover and exploit vulnerabilities in Internet of Things systems, an application domain that had not previously been explored for this type of AI-driven security testing. The researchers developed a multi-agent framework called VEXAIoT, which pairs a vulnerability detection agent with an attack execution agent to conduct reconnaissance, plan attack sequences, and carry out exploits against IoT services using LLM-based reasoning combined with offensive security tools. The framework was evaluated across ten attack scenarios mapped to established OWASP IoT vulnerability categories within two emulated environments, IoTGoat and Metasploitable. Results showed a high overall attack success rate with low computational overhead and fast execution times, demonstrating that LLM-driven agents can effectively automate IoT vulnerability assessment workflows in controlled settings.
items.citation: Katherine Swinea, Kshitiz Aryal, Lopamudra Praharaj et al.. VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Biochemical and Biotechnological Approaches to Disease Diagnosis and Therapeutics: A Comprehensive Review.
items.summary: This review examines how advances in biochemical and biotechnological sciences have reshaped disease diagnosis and treatment across oncological, infectious, metabolic, and degenerative conditions. The authors synthesised key innovations in biochemical diagnostics and biotechnology-driven therapeutics, evaluating their clinical relevance and contributions to precision medicine. The review finds that recent developments have meaningfully improved early disease identification, molecular characterisation, and targeted treatment strategies, while also enabling minimally invasive monitoring and more personalised clinical decision-making. The authors conclude that biochemical and biotechnological innovations are central to precision medicine's progress, but stress that continued validation, optimisation, and integration into healthcare systems will be necessary to sustain improved clinical outcomes.
items.citation: Kadwe P, Jena L, Jena D et al.. Biochemical and Biotechnological Approaches to Disease Diagnosis and Therapeutics: A Comprehensive Review.. Cureus. 2026

items.title: Noninvasive SARS-CoV-2 detection using a low-cost electronic nose.
items.summary: This study investigated whether a low-cost, noninvasive electronic nose could detect SARS-CoV-2 as an alternative to conventional diagnostic methods that are often uncomfortable and slow. The device used an array of commercially available metal-oxide gas sensors to capture and analyze volatile organic compounds in both saliva and exhaled breath, which shift in composition in the presence of the virus. Several machine learning algorithms were then applied to classify samples as infected or non-infected, including K-Nearest Neighbors, Support Vector Machine, Neural Networks, and Random Forest. Performance varied across algorithms and sample types, with exhaled breath yielding slightly higher discriminatory power than saliva, and the K-Nearest Neighbors algorithm achieving the best results for both matrices. The authors conclude that these findings provide proof-of-concept support for the feasibility of using a low-cost electronic nose for point-of-care SARS-CoV-2 detection in a real-world hospital setting.
items.venue: The Brazilian journal of infectious diseases : an official publication of the Brazilian Society of Infectious Diseases
items.citation: Fialkovitz G, Sousa PL, Hidifira AM et al.. Noninvasive SARS-CoV-2 detection using a low-cost electronic nose.. The Brazilian journal of infectious diseases : an official publication of the Brazilian Society of Infectious Diseases. 2026

items.title: Multiniche mycobiome profiling identifies distinctive fungal dysbiosis in common variable immunodeficiency.
items.summary: This study investigated the fungal microbiome (mycobiome) in patients with common variable immunodeficiency (CVID), a condition known for bacterial dysbiosis but poorly characterized in terms of its fungal communities. The researchers performed a cross-sectional, multi-compartment analysis comparing saliva, sputum, and stool samples from adults with CVID—subdivided into those with immune dysregulation and those with infectious-only manifestations—against healthy controls, using amplicon sequencing and machine learning approaches. Across all three body sites, CVID patients showed significantly altered mycobiome composition relative to controls, characterized by reduced fungal richness and evenness, particularly in respiratory and oral samples. A consistent pattern of enrichment in opportunistic yeasts, especially *Candida albicans*, was observed across niches in both CVID subtypes, while commensal and environmental fungi were depleted, suggesting a potential pathobiont role for *C. albicans*. Fungal community profiles demonstrated strong discriminatory power in distinguishing CVID patients from healthy controls, supporting their potential utility as non-invasive biomarkers for this immunodeficiency.
items.citation: Cabanero-Navalon MD, Carda-Diéguez M, Mira A et al.. Multiniche mycobiome profiling identifies distinctive fungal dysbiosis in common variable immunodeficiency.. Frontiers in immunology. 2026

items.title: Salivary exosomal <i>Mycobacterium tuberculosis</i> DNA enables sensitive detection of paucibacillary tuberculosis: a molecular diagnosis study.
items.summary: This study investigated whether *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* DNA extracted from salivary exosomes could serve as a non-sputum-based method for diagnosing paucibacillary tuberculosis, a form of the disease that is particularly difficult to detect. The researchers developed an exosomal nucleic acid (ExoNA) diagnostic platform that combines salivary exosome enrichment, nucleic acid extraction, and dual-target quantitative PCR melting curve analysis, reducing turnaround time substantially compared to conventional approaches. In a pilot clinical study enrolling adults with suspected pulmonary tuberculosis—the majority of whom had negative smear microscopy results—the assay demonstrated moderate sensitivity and specificity against both standard and extended microbiological reference standards, with sensitivity maintained in the smear-negative subgroup. The findings establish salivary exosomal DNA as the predominant detectable form of *M. tuberculosis* DNA in saliva and support the feasibility of this non-invasive, rapid diagnostic approach. The authors conclude that multicenter validation in more diverse populations is needed before the assay can be adopted for routine clinical use.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Ma J, Zheng X, He Y et al.. Salivary exosomal <i>Mycobacterium tuberculosis</i> DNA enables sensitive detection of paucibacillary tuberculosis: a molecular diagnosis study.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis
items.summary: This paper introduces a topological signal processing framework called PHINN-EEG for analyzing EEG recordings associated with dream states, addressing limitations of conventional approaches that rely on power spectral density and statistical moment features. The method applies sliding-window Takens delay embeddings and Vietoris-Rips filtrations to multichannel pre-awakening EEG epochs, extracting so-called Dynamic Betti Curves that capture the geometric and topological structure of neural activity rather than its spectral energy content. These topological invariants are combined with topology-conditioned flow matching for dream-state EEG synthesis, and the framework also includes a spectral-conditioned flow model as an ablation baseline to isolate the contribution of topological conditioning specifically. The authors analytically project their approach to substantially outperform existing benchmarks on an open-access dream database, and they propose candidate Betti transition archetypes hypothesized to link phase-space topology to phenomenological dream report categories, though these remain to be empirically validated. The work is presented as a conceptual and methodological proposal rather than a fully validated empirical study, framing a potential paradigm shift from spectral to geometric characterization of neural rare-event detection.
items.citation: Ren Takahashi, Emre Yusuf, Jayabrata Bhaduri. PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Impact of Benign Connectivity Variations on Intrusion Detection for Encrypted OPC UA Traffic in Industrial Private 5G Networks
items.summary: This paper examined how machine learning-based intrusion detection systems behave when monitoring encrypted industrial communication traffic under realistic private 5G network conditions. The researchers specifically investigated whether normal, benign connectivity variations in industrial private 5G networks affect the performance of intrusion detection systems designed for encrypted OPC UA traffic. Their experiments revealed that legitimate connectivity events can substantially increase false positive rates even in the complete absence of any actual attacks. Additionally, elevated anomaly scores from the intrusion detection system were found to frequently align with control-plane activity associated with these benign connectivity events. The findings underscore the need to incorporate control-plane context when interpreting intrusion detection outputs in industrial private 5G environments to avoid mischaracterizing normal network behavior as malicious.
items.citation: Song Son Ha, Florian Foerster, Henry Beuster et al.. Impact of Benign Connectivity Variations on Intrusion Detection for Encrypted OPC UA Traffic in Industrial Private 5G Networks. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Identifying and characterizing extragalactic circum-CBC exoplanets with future gravitational-wave detectors
items.summary: This paper investigates whether exoplanets orbiting compact binary coalescence systems outside the Milky Way could be detected using future gravitational-wave observatories. The researchers applied a technique that recovers information about an external gravitational potential by analyzing how it causes the binary's center of mass to wobble around the common barycenter, leaving a characteristic imprint on the emitted gravitational-wave signal. They examined a range of circum-compact-binary exoplanet configurations in both circular and eccentric orbits for systems detectable by next-generation instruments including an upgraded LIGO, Einstein Telescope, and DECIGO. The study found that for a substantial portion of the parameter space considered, the presence of such an exoplanet can be identified and its mass recovered to within an order-unity factor of the true value, albeit with a degeneracy introduced by the unknown orbital inclination angle. This work constitutes a proof-of-principle demonstration that gravitational waves offer a novel channel for discovering the first extragalactic exoplanets.
items.citation: Avinash Tiwari, Aditya Vijaykumar, Shasvath J. Kapadia et al.. Identifying and characterizing extragalactic circum-CBC exoplanets with future gravitational-wave detectors. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models
items.summary: This paper examined how vision-language models have progressed in describing complex visual scenes over roughly a decade, with particular attention to the types of errors these models make. The researchers introduced a new benchmark dataset containing images of complex social interactions and behaviors, which they used alongside a standard simpler scene dataset to evaluate both models and human describers against a gold standard. They assessed nine models spanning pre-multimodal large language model and multimodal large language model generations across five error categories: object detection, recognition, hallucination, scene understanding, and spatial dependence. The study found that earlier models performed far below human-level accuracy on complex social scenes, whereas recent multimodal large language models reached accuracy levels comparable to top-performing human describers and effectively closed the performance gap between simple and complex scenes. Nearly all error types were largely eliminated by the latest models, with the exception of spatial dependence errors, and detection, recognition, and hallucination errors were identified as having the greatest overall impact on description accuracy.
items.citation: Shravan Murlidaran, Miguel P. Eckstein. Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models. arXiv. 2026

items.title: VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents
items.summary: This paper investigated whether large language model-based AI agents could autonomously discover and exploit vulnerabilities in Internet of Things systems, an application domain that had not previously been explored for this type of automated security testing. The researchers developed a multi-agent framework called VEXAIoT, consisting of a vulnerability detection agent and an attack execution agent that together perform reconnaissance, plan attack sequences, and execute exploits against IoT services using LLM reasoning combined with offensive security tools. The system was evaluated across ten attack scenarios mapped to established OWASP IoT vulnerability categories within two controlled testbed environments, IoTGoat and Metasploitable. Results showed that the framework achieved a very high overall attack success rate with low computational overhead and fast execution times across hundreds of individual attack runs. The findings suggest that LLM-driven multi-agent systems can effectively automate IoT vulnerability assessment workflows in controlled settings, raising both promise for scalable security testing and implicit concerns about the accessibility of such offensive capabilities.
items.citation: Katherine Swinea, Kshitiz Aryal, Lopamudra Praharaj et al.. VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Silicon-Germanium Heterostructures with Enhanced Valley Splitting for Spin Qubits
items.summary: The paper investigates how to achieve large and consistent valley splittings in silicon-germanium heterostructures used for gate-defined spin qubits, since insufficient valley splitting causes errors in qubit initialization, operation, and readout. The authors developed a device-level optimization strategy using device-scale simulations that incorporate atomistic alloy disorder through a one-dimensional tight-binding theory, supplemented by atomistic density functional theory calculations. Their proposed heterostructure design combines an unconventional combination of a narrow quantum well, a small germanium spike, and a pure-germanium cap to maximize valley splitting. This approach is predicted to yield valley splittings well beyond those reported in nearly all prior theoretical work, while also producing a tight statistical distribution across different disorder configurations—a critical requirement for reproducible performance across many qubits. The authors argue that if experimentally realized, this design would effectively eliminate valley splitting as a fundamental obstacle to scaling up silicon-germanium quantum processors.
items.citation: David W. Kanaar, Efrain Martinez, Peihong Zhang et al.. Silicon-Germanium Heterostructures with Enhanced Valley Splitting for Spin Qubits. arXiv. 2026
