items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review paper examines the current landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the global need for decentralized, rapid, and accessible testing. The authors provide a comprehensive overview organized around the three core operational steps of such diagnostics: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection. For extraction, they discuss approaches including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic methods that prioritize simplicity and portability. For amplification, they review how established techniques such as PCR alongside isothermal methods including LAMP, RPA, and NASBA have been adapted for low-power and field-deployable conditions. Finally, for detection, the review covers a spectrum from colorimetric and fluorescent lateral flow assays to advanced biosensor platforms exploiting CRISPR-Cas systems and toehold switch principles, highlighting their capacity for sensitive and programmable nucleic acid recognition.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

query: group A streptococcus molecular diagnostic test

items.title: Performance of the Xpert Xpress Strep A for detection of <i>Streptococcus pyogenes</i> from pediatric pleural fluids.
items.summary: This study evaluated the performance of a rapid molecular assay, Xpert Xpress Strep A, for detecting *Streptococcus pyogenes* (Group A Streptococcus) in pediatric pleural fluid specimens, a clinically important application given that recovery of the pathogen from a sterile site is required to classify disease as invasive. The researchers compared the assay against bacterial culture and a composite reference standard comprising positive culture combined with a laboratory-developed quantitative PCR targeting a different gene. Testing a convenience sample of pediatric pleural fluids that included both confirmed positive and negative specimens, the Xpert assay detected all positive samples, achieving complete agreement with the composite reference standard, while culture alone identified only a minority of confirmed positive cases. These findings demonstrate that the Xpert assay is substantially more sensitive than culture for detecting Group A Streptococcus from pleural fluid, likely reflecting the impact of prior antibiotic treatment on culture yield. The authors conclude that Xpert offers both superior sensitivity and faster turnaround time compared to culture, supporting its diagnostic utility for guiding timely therapy and public health management of invasive Group A streptococcal disease.
items.citation: Amaral G, Gadkar VJ, Almodahka A et al.. Performance of the Xpert Xpress Strep A for detection of <i>Streptococcus pyogenes</i> from pediatric pleural fluids.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: Phase-switchable nonreciprocal entanglement via magnon squeezing in ring-cavity optomagnomechanics
items.summary: This paper theoretically investigates how phase-controlled magnon squeezing can generate and switch quantum entanglement in a ring-cavity optomagnomechanical system. The proposed scheme couples two spatially separated ferrimagnetic yttrium iron garnet microbridges through magnetostriction-mediated mechanical motion and a shared cavity field driven by radiation pressure. The squeezing process introduces phase-dependent contributions to the magnon response, specifically an effective detuning shift and a quadrature-damping term, both of which reverse sign under a phase shift of π, enabling in situ control over the entanglement. Nonreciprocity is defined through the asymmetric entanglement response under this phase reversal and quantified by normalized contrast ratios comparing entanglement at a given phase versus its phase-reversed configuration. The authors find that this phase-tuning approach provides a flexible and robust mechanism for achieving high-contrast switchable bipartite and tripartite entanglement, establishing magnon squeezing as a practical quantum resource in hybrid quantum platforms.
items.citation: Z. Imara, A. El Allati, A. Belfakir et al.. Phase-switchable nonreciprocal entanglement via magnon squeezing in ring-cavity optomagnomechanics. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Identifying and characterizing extragalactic circum-CBC exoplanets with future gravitational-wave detectors
items.summary: This paper investigates whether exoplanets orbiting compact binary coalescences (CBCs) — such as binary neutron stars, neutron star-black hole systems, and binary black holes — could be detected in other galaxies using future gravitational-wave detectors. The authors exploit the fact that a circum-CBC exoplanet causes the binary's centre of mass to wobble around the shared barycentre, imprinting a characteristic signature on the emitted gravitational-wave signal. By reconstructing the centre-of-mass kinematics encoded in the waveform, they extract information about the external gravitational potential provided by the orbiting planet. Testing this approach with example systems detectable by next-generation instruments including Einstein Telescope and DECIGO, and considering a range of planetary orbital parameters in both circular and eccentric configurations, they find that for a substantial fraction of the parameter space explored, the presence of a circum-CBC exoplanet can be identified and its mass recovered to within an order-unity factor of the true value at reasonable confidence. This would represent the first method capable of detecting exoplanets beyond the Milky Way.
items.citation: Avinash Tiwari, Aditya Vijaykumar, Shasvath J. Kapadia et al.. Identifying and characterizing extragalactic circum-CBC exoplanets with future gravitational-wave detectors. arXiv. 2026

items.title: HD 148797: A bright F-type star with two moderate-period low-density sub-Jovian planets. Compact multi-planet architectures are common in the Neptunian savanna
items.summary: The paper reports the discovery and characterization of two transiting sub-Jovian planets orbiting the bright F-type star HD 148797, using photometric data from the TESS and CHEOPS space missions. The two planets have orbital periods of roughly 42 and 68 days, radii near 8.3 Earth radii each, and masses around 39 Earth masses each, derived from photodynamical modeling of anti-correlated transit-timing variations. Their low bulk densities place both planets in the sparsely populated Neptunian savanna, a region of parameter space where such objects are relatively rare. By examining the broader population of savanna systems, the authors find that multi-planet configurations are common in this regime, with most systems containing at least one adjacent planet pair with an orbital period ratio below three, suggesting these planets arrived through smooth disk-driven migration rather than disruptive high-eccentricity tidal migration. The system's brightness and the similar properties of its two planets make it a valuable target for comparative atmospheric studies.
items.citation: J. M. Almenara, A. Castro-González, R. Mardling et al.. HD 148797: A bright F-type star with two moderate-period low-density sub-Jovian planets. Compact multi-planet architectures are common in the Neptunian savanna. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models
items.summary: This paper examined how vision-language models have improved in describing complex visual scenes over roughly a decade of development. The researchers introduced a new benchmark dataset containing images of complex social interactions and behaviors, using it alongside an established simpler scene dataset to evaluate both model accuracy and specific types of visual-cognitive errors across nine models spanning pre-multimodal and multimodal large language model eras. Accuracy was assessed relative to a gold standard and compared against human descriptions, while five error categories were tracked: object detection, recognition, hallucination, scene understanding, and spatial dependence. The study found that earlier models performed far below even the lowest-ranked human descriptions on complex scenes, whereas recent multimodal large language models reached accuracy levels comparable to top human descriptions and effectively closed the performance gap between simple and complex scene types. Nearly all error types were substantially reduced by modern models, with spatial dependence errors proving most persistent, and detection, recognition, and hallucination errors shown to have the greatest impact on overall description accuracy.
items.citation: Shravan Murlidaran, Miguel P. Eckstein. Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models. arXiv. 2026

items.title: VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents
items.summary: This paper investigated whether large language model-based AI agents could autonomously discover and exploit vulnerabilities in Internet of Things systems, an area previously unexplored despite growing interest in AI-driven penetration testing. The researchers developed a multi-agent framework called VEXAIoT, combining a vulnerability detection agent and an attack execution agent to perform reconnaissance, plan attack sequences, and execute exploits against IoT services using LLM reasoning and offensive security tools. The system was evaluated across ten attack scenarios mapped to OWASP IoT vulnerabilities using two established testbed environments, IoTGoat and Metasploitable. The framework achieved a very high overall attack success rate across hundreds of executions, with most attacks completing in under two minutes and low computational overhead in terms of token usage. The findings suggest that LLM-driven multi-agent systems can effectively automate IoT vulnerability assessment and offensive security workflows in controlled environments.
items.citation: Katherine Swinea, Kshitiz Aryal, Lopamudra Praharaj et al.. VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: B-spline Policy: Accelerating Manipulation Policies via B-spline Action Representations
items.summary: This paper introduces B-spline Policy (BSP), an action representation framework designed to speed up robot manipulation by parameterizing actions as continuous B-spline curves rather than discrete action chunks. The key idea is to define trajectories through a set of knots and control points, producing smooth, time-continuous paths that can be temporally scaled and executed by low-level controllers at higher frequencies than standard policy outputs. BSP is designed to integrate into existing policy learning pipelines by training models to directly predict B-spline parameters instead of discrete actions. Evaluations on both simulated and real-world manipulation tasks show that BSP substantially reduces task completion time compared to baseline methods while preserving high task success rates.
items.citation: Xiaoshen Han, Haoyu Xiong, Haonan Chen et al.. B-spline Policy: Accelerating Manipulation Policies via B-spline Action Representations. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Innovative epitope-based ELISA assay for detecting Group B Streptococcus carriage in pregnant women.
items.summary: The paper studied whether synthetic peptide epitopes derived from Group B Streptococcus (Streptococcus agalactiae) surface proteins could serve as the basis for an ELISA-based immunodiagnostic test to detect GBS infection and carriage in pregnant women. Three epitopes were selected from the bacterial proteins GroEL, enolase, and EF-Tu, and tested individually and in combination against serum samples from GBS-positive and GBS-negative subjects, measuring IgG, IgM, and IgA antibody responses. All peptides, whether used alone or mixed, significantly distinguished between positive and negative sera, with the enolase-derived and EF-Tu-derived epitopes performing best individually due to lower required concentrations and higher absorbance values in positive samples. Combining the epitopes in various mixtures further improved immunoreactivity beyond that achieved by any single peptide. The authors propose this prototype epitope-based ELISA as a promising alternative to the current gold-standard culture method, which is time-consuming and can yield ambiguous results.
items.citation: Brzychczy-Włoch M, Dobrut A, Drożdż K et al.. Innovative epitope-based ELISA assay for detecting Group B Streptococcus carriage in pregnant women.. Applied microbiology and biotechnology. 2026

items.title: Bridging the diagnostic gap: the role of culture methods in the era of syndromic panels for central nervous system infections.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a syndromic molecular panel for central nervous system infections by comparing it against conventional culture methods using cerebrospinal fluid samples collected over three years at a national reference center. The researchers assessed concordance, sensitivity, specificity, and predictive values across a large dataset of paired molecular and culture results. They found moderate overall agreement between the two approaches, with the syndromic panel demonstrating high specificity but limited sensitivity when all culture-detected pathogens were considered; however, when analysis was restricted to bacterial targets actually covered by the panel, both agreement and sensitivity improved substantially. Critically, conventional culture identified clinically relevant pathogens not included in the syndromic panel, such as certain staphylococcal and streptococcal species as well as rare environmental organisms, underscoring gaps in panel coverage. The authors conclude that syndromic molecular panels cannot serve as standalone diagnostic tools and must be systematically combined with conventional culture to ensure comprehensive pathogen detection and guide appropriate antimicrobial management.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Sberna G, D'Arezzo S, Valli MB et al.. Bridging the diagnostic gap: the role of culture methods in the era of syndromic panels for central nervous system infections.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: Performance of the LIAISON PLEX gram-positive blood culture assay for identifying bacterial pathogens and resistance genes in blood cultures.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of the LIAISON PLEX Gram-Positive Blood Culture Assay, a multiplexed molecular test designed to rapidly identify gram-positive bacterial pathogens and antibiotic resistance markers directly from positive blood culture bottles. The assay was assessed across multiple sites using a combination of prospective clinical samples, pre-selected samples, and contrived specimens, encompassing detection of thirteen gram-positive bacterial targets and four antibiotic resistance markers. The assay achieved very high overall sensitivity and specificity across all targets, with minimal cross-reactivity observed among off-panel bacterial species and no meaningful interference from tested substances. The assay demonstrated compatibility with blood culture bottles from multiple manufacturers and maintained strong performance in the context of polymicrobial infections. The authors conclude that the assay offers fast, accurate, and reliable detection of gram-positive organisms and their resistance genes from positive blood cultures, with potential to support antibiotic optimization and patient management decisions.
items.citation: Chow S-K, Stevig C, Granato P et al.. Performance of the LIAISON PLEX gram-positive blood culture assay for identifying bacterial pathogens and resistance genes in blood cultures.. Journal of clinical microbiology. 2026

items.title: Validation of an in-house developed rapid assay for Group B Streptococcus detection in pregnancy.
items.summary: This study addressed the need for a rapid and reliable method to detect Group B Streptococcus colonization in pregnant women, a key risk factor for neonatal infections and preterm stillbirth. The researchers developed an in-house assay combining recombinase polymerase amplification with the CRISPR/Cas12a system, enabling isothermal amplification and detection without complex equipment. The assay was validated for specificity against multiple reproductive tract pathogens, and its detection limit was established using serially diluted plasmid standards. Clinical performance was evaluated using vaginal-rectal swabs from pregnant women, with results compared against a commercial quantitative real-time PCR method, and the rapid assay showed close concordance with this established reference. The authors conclude that the assay, which completes testing within one hour and allows visual or fluorescence-based result interpretation, demonstrates high sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy with strong potential for clinical deployment.
items.venue: International journal of medical microbiology : IJMM
items.citation: Hu S, Xie J, Liu L et al.. Validation of an in-house developed rapid assay for Group B Streptococcus detection in pregnancy.. International journal of medical microbiology : IJMM. 2026

items.title: Utility and clinical relevance of multiplex PCR panels for diagnosis of central nervous system infections.
items.summary: This study evaluated the real-world diagnostic performance and clinical utility of multiplex PCR panels designed to detect multiple pathogens simultaneously from cerebrospinal fluid in patients with suspected meningitis or encephalitis. Researchers retrospectively reviewed cerebrospinal fluid samples tested over a five-year period at a tertiary care hospital, examining test results, pathogen detection rates, co-detection events, and whether positive results led to documented changes in diagnosis or treatment. Viral pathogens dominated the positive findings, with human herpesvirus-6, enterovirus, and herpes simplex virus type-1 being most frequently identified, while Streptococcus pneumoniae was the most common bacterial detection. Despite the technology's capacity for rapid, broad pathogen identification, fewer than half of positive results were associated with a clinically relevant action, repeat testing after an initial negative result yielded little additional diagnostic information, and some panels were ordered for conditions outside their intended scope. The authors conclude that while multiplex PCR panels are a valuable diagnostic tool for central nervous system infections, their clinical impact is undermined by inappropriate utilisation and insufficient clinician familiarity with panel contents, highlighting the need for clearer testing criteria and improved stewardship.
items.venue: The Indian journal of medical research
items.citation: Gürler M, Karaoğlu Ç, Yurtcu EE et al.. Utility and clinical relevance of multiplex PCR panels for diagnosis of central nervous system infections.. The Indian journal of medical research. 2026

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review paper examines the landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the global need for rapid, decentralized testing outside conventional laboratory settings. The authors systematically survey the three core operational steps of such diagnostics: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection. For extraction, they evaluate approaches including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic methods with an emphasis on simplicity and equipment-free operation. Amplification strategies reviewed include both thermal cycling methods such as PCR and isothermal alternatives such as LAMP, RPA, and NASBA, all adapted for portable and low-power conditions. On the detection side, the review spans colorimetric and fluorescent lateral flow assays through to advanced biosensor platforms exploiting CRISPR/Cas systems and toehold switch principles, which together offer highly sensitive and programmable nucleic acid recognition suitable for point-of-care deployment.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

items.title: A Novel Voltammetric Point-of-Care Device for Rapid and Accurate Nucleic Acid and Viral Pathogen Detection.
items.summary: This paper developed and evaluated an electrochemical nucleic acid amplification test that combines isothermal amplification with real-time voltammetric detection for infectious disease diagnostics. The approach works by monitoring protons released during amplification using a pH-sensing redox compound, offering an alternative to optical detection methods that can complicate existing isothermal platforms. The system was tested against a range of respiratory viral pathogens, including SARS-CoV-2, influenza A and B viruses, RSV A, and equine herpesvirus, using both purified RNA from clinical samples and an extraction-free workflow. Results showed high concordance with RT-qPCR across these pathogens and sample types, along with a low limit of detection for influenza A virus. The authors conclude that real-time voltammetric LAMP is feasible across multiple sample matrices, though broader validation in additional contexts and prospective cohorts is still needed.
items.citation: Munita R, Lyttleton R, Danefur E et al.. A Novel Voltammetric Point-of-Care Device for Rapid and Accurate Nucleic Acid and Viral Pathogen Detection.. ACS omega. 2026

items.title: Isothermal nucleic acid amplification techniques: A comprehensive overview on their principle & applications as alternative to PCR.
items.summary: This review paper examined isothermal nucleic acid amplification methods as alternatives to conventional PCR-based diagnostics. The authors comprehensively surveyed the principles, detection strategies, and applications of the most widely used techniques, including loop-mediated isothermal amplification, helicase-dependent amplification, rolling circle amplification, strand displacement amplification, nucleic acid sequence-based amplification, and recombinase polymerase amplification. Because these methods operate at a constant temperature without requiring a thermal cycler, they offer simplified workflows and reduced costs particularly suited to resource-limited settings. The review synthesizes information on reaction mechanisms, product detection approaches, and applications in disease diagnosis that had previously been scattered across the literature. The authors conclude that isothermal amplification techniques represent a promising and accessible avenue for expanding molecular diagnostics in both research and clinical practice, and they discuss future perspectives for the field.
items.citation: Amare Amdiyee A, Sisay Tessema T. Isothermal nucleic acid amplification techniques: A comprehensive overview on their principle & applications as alternative to PCR.. Practical laboratory medicine. 2026

items.title: A nested PCR-based point-of-care multiplex test for detection of bacterial pathogens in cerebrospinal fluids.
items.summary: This study developed and evaluated a multiplex one-tube nested real-time PCR assay integrated with a point-of-care testing instrument for detecting three bacterial pathogens—*Streptococcus pneumoniae*, *Streptococcus agalactiae*, and *Streptococcus pyogenes*—in cerebrospinal fluid. The approach was designed to achieve a fully automated sample-to-result workflow without requiring conventional culture methods. The assay demonstrated high sensitivity with very low detection limits, no cross-reactivity with other common pathogens, and successfully detected all three targets in simulated cerebrospinal fluid samples within approximately two hours. When evaluated on clinical samples, the point-of-care system identified additional positive cases that conventional real-time PCR had missed, indicating superior sensitivity. The authors conclude that this integrated system offers a rapid, sensitive, and operationally streamlined approach to diagnosing bacterial central nervous system infections at the point of care.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Wu J, Zhao Z, Pang P et al.. A nested PCR-based point-of-care multiplex test for detection of bacterial pathogens in cerebrospinal fluids.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.
items.summary: This review examined the landscape of molecular diagnostic technologies for bacterial infections, with particular emphasis on improving the speed and accuracy of etiological diagnosis in the context of rising antimicrobial resistance. The authors conducted a structured comparative analysis of major platforms including PCR and its variants, isothermal amplification, next-generation sequencing, CRISPR-based diagnostics, and digital PCR, evaluating each against key performance parameters such as sensitivity, specificity, limit of detection, turnaround time, and multiplexing capacity. The review also critically assessed the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning into diagnostic workflows for resistance prediction and clinical decision support. Additionally, the translational feasibility of these technologies for point-of-care deployment was considered, accounting for clinical validation requirements and real-world operational constraints. The authors conclude that advancing culture-independent bacteriological diagnostics requires careful balancing of analytical performance with clinical interpretability, and they outline remaining challenges and future directions for the field.
items.citation: Cotelici AM, Bălășoiu AT, Boldeanu MV et al.. Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.. International journal of molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: A Multiplex One-Tube Nested Real-Time PCR Assay for the Point-of-Care Testing of Infectious Meningitis.
items.summary: This paper developed a multiplex one-tube nested real-time PCR assay integrated with a portable, fully automated point-of-care testing platform for detecting three key meningitis pathogens in cerebrospinal fluid: *Haemophilus influenzae*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, and *Cryptococcus neoformans*. The approach performs nested amplification within a closed system to reduce contamination risk and operational complexity, and was evaluated analytically for sensitivity, specificity, and cross-reactivity before being tested on clinical samples. The assay demonstrated very low detection limits, no cross-reactivity against a broad panel of non-target organisms, and delivered results in under two hours. Clinical evaluation using cerebrospinal fluid samples showed near-perfect agreement with conventional quantitative PCR, and the platform detected additional *C. neoformans* cases missed by standard methods, suggesting superior sensitivity in clinical conditions. The authors conclude that the assay offers a rapid, sensitive, and practical approach for point-of-care meningitis diagnosis, though they note the need for validation in larger patient cohorts.
items.citation: Zhang D, Wang J, Zhao Z et al.. A Multiplex One-Tube Nested Real-Time PCR Assay for the Point-of-Care Testing of Infectious Meningitis.. Pathogens (Basel, Switzerland). 2026

items.title: Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models
items.summary: This paper examined how vision-language models have progressed over a decade in their ability to describe visually complex scenes involving human social interactions. The researchers introduced a new benchmark dataset of images depicting complex social behaviors and evaluated nine models spanning from early pre-multimodal systems to recent large multimodal language models, comparing their outputs against human descriptions and a gold standard. They assessed five categories of visual-cognitive errors, including object detection, hallucination, and spatial dependence, across both this new dataset and a standard simpler benchmark. The findings showed that earlier models performed far below even the weakest human describers, while recent multimodal large language models reached accuracy levels comparable to top-performing humans and effectively closed the gap between simple and complex scene description tasks. Most error types were nearly eliminated by modern models, with the exception of spatial dependence errors, and detection, recognition, and hallucination errors were identified as having the greatest influence on overall description accuracy.
items.citation: Shravan Murlidaran, Miguel P. Eckstein. Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models. arXiv. 2026

items.title: VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents
items.summary: This paper investigated whether large language model-based AI agents could autonomously discover and exploit vulnerabilities in Internet of Things systems, an area that had not previously been explored despite growing use of such agents in other cybersecurity contexts. The researchers developed a multi-agent framework called VEXAIoT, combining a vulnerability detection agent and an attack execution agent that together perform reconnaissance, plan attack sequences, and execute exploits against IoT services using offensive security tooling. The framework was evaluated across ten attack scenarios mapped to established IoT vulnerability categories in two controlled testbed environments. Results showed a very high overall attack success rate with low computational overhead and fast execution times across hundreds of attack runs. The findings demonstrate that autonomous, LLM-driven agents can effectively automate IoT vulnerability assessment and offensive security workflows, highlighting both the promise of this approach for security testing and the potential risks posed by such capable automated systems.
items.citation: Katherine Swinea, Kshitiz Aryal, Lopamudra Praharaj et al.. VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Spatial decomposition of Little Red Dots with JWST/NIRSpec IFU into broad-line red cores and narrow-line blue host galaxies
items.summary: This paper investigates the physical nature of Little Red Dots, a class of compact red objects discovered by JWST that display puzzling spectral features including a V-shaped continuum, broad Balmer emission lines, and in some cases Balmer absorption. To test whether these features originate from two distinct physical components — a central engine hosting a rapidly growing black hole and a surrounding host galaxy — the authors used JWST/NIRSpec integral field unit spectroscopy to spatially map different spectral components across five broad Hα-selected Little Red Dots at a redshift of approximately five. By decomposing the continuum, narrow and broad emission lines, and absorption features spatially, they found that the blue continuum emission is co-spatial with the narrow emission line region, while the red continuum occupies a compact core coincident with the broad Balmer features. Spatial maps of the oxygen equivalent width further revealed a marked decrease at the central core, consistent with distinct emission mechanisms in the two regions. These findings provide strong observational support for a two-component model in which a red central engine is embedded within a blue host galaxy.
items.citation: Yuzo Ishikawa, Anna-Christina Eilers, Rohan P. Naidu et al.. Spatial decomposition of Little Red Dots with JWST/NIRSpec IFU into broad-line red cores and narrow-line blue host galaxies. arXiv. 2026

items.title: SPOT-IC: Improving prediction for interval-censored data via survival probability transfer
items.summary: This paper addresses the problem of accurately predicting survival outcomes when data are interval-censored, a challenge that becomes particularly acute with wide censoring intervals and limited follow-up typical of chronic disease studies. The authors developed a transfer learning method called SPOT-IC that borrows survival probability information from external source studies to improve estimation in a target study, without requiring access to individual-level source data or imposing strict assumptions about model similarity between studies. The method uses a penalty-based framework and an EM algorithm for efficient computation, and includes a data-adaptive aggregation procedure that guards against negative transfer when multiple source studies of uncertain quality are available. Theoretically, the proposed estimator achieves faster convergence than an estimator using only the target study data, provided at least one source study is sufficiently informative. The approach was validated through simulation studies and applied to data from an Alzheimer's disease neuroimaging cohort, demonstrating its practical effectiveness.
items.citation: Jingyi Hu, Yu Gu. SPOT-IC: Improving prediction for interval-censored data via survival probability transfer. arXiv. 2026

items.title: A Quantum Path to Partial Differential Equations
items.summary: These lecture notes address the use of fault-tolerant quantum algorithms for solving partial differential equations, a topic that sits at the intersection of numerical analysis and quantum computation. The work uses block encoding as a central organizing principle, showing how discretized differential operators can be embedded in unitaries and then processed using quantum primitives such as quantum singular value transformation, Hamiltonian simulation, and amplitude amplification. Each topic is developed starting from standard finite difference or finite element discretizations and follows the complete pipeline from the continuous problem through quantum encoding, transformation, and extraction of quantities of interest. The treatment carefully examines end-to-end performance factors including discretization error, state preparation, normalization, and measurement cost for elliptic, hyperbolic, and parabolic equations, and concludes by extending the framework to nonlinear problems via Carleman and Koopman–von Neumann linearizations. The explicit goal is to provide a mathematically transparent entry point and a common vocabulary for researchers from both communities rather than to assert universal quantum advantage.
items.citation: Xiantao Li. A Quantum Path to Partial Differential Equations. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Lean-QIT: Towards a Formal Infrastructure for Quantum Information Theory
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of formally verifying quantum information theory (QIT) within a machine-checked proof framework, noting that existing approaches lack a reusable operational layer capable of unifying finite-block protocols, analytic inequalities, and asymptotic limits. The authors developed Lean-QIT, a library built in the Lean 4 proof assistant that provides composable, kernel-checked interfaces for quantum states and channels, coding constructs, performance criteria, hypothesis testing, and asymptotic rate constructions. Using this infrastructure, they formally verified several foundational results in QIT, including Schumacher's quantum source-coding theorem, the Holevo–Schumacher–Westmoreland classical-capacity theorem, and the entanglement-assisted classical-capacity theorem along with its strong converse. The library separates operational definitions from analytic characterizations and exposes reusable achievability, converse, and asymptotic components, creating a modular and extensible formal foundation. The authors claim this framework can serve as both a machine-readable substrate for formal QIT and a compositional knowledge base suitable for AI-assisted formalization and automated proof search in quantum information science.
items.citation: Chengkai Zhu, Ziao Tang, Guocheng Zhen et al.. Lean-QIT: Towards a Formal Infrastructure for Quantum Information Theory. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Evaluation of the QuickFinder COVID-19/Flu/RSV antigen test for rapid simultaneous detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza A/B viruses, and respiratory syncytial virus.
items.summary: This study evaluated a lateral flow immunoassay-based antigen rapid diagnostic test designed to simultaneously detect SARS-CoV-2, influenza A, influenza B, and respiratory syncytial virus from nasopharyngeal swab specimens. The test was assessed against real-time reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction as the reference standard using archived clinical specimens, and cross-reactivity was examined against a panel of respiratory viral and bacterial strains. The test demonstrated high sensitivity for all four targets, particularly in specimens with high viral loads, and achieved perfect specificity with no observed cross-reactivity against the tested microorganisms. Performance declined in specimens with low viral loads, leading the authors to conclude that the test is best suited as a complementary rapid screening tool rather than a replacement for molecular diagnostics.
items.citation: Kim JA, Kang M, Yu HJ et al.. Evaluation of the QuickFinder COVID-19/Flu/RSV antigen test for rapid simultaneous detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza A/B viruses, and respiratory syncytial virus.. Diagnostic microbiology and infectious disease. 2026

items.title: No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients
items.summary: This study investigated the disease burden of respiratory syncytial virus infection among adult patients at a Japanese clinic by testing nasopharyngeal swabs collected from individuals aged eighteen and older presenting with acute respiratory infection symptoms. Rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests capable of simultaneously detecting SARS-CoV-2, influenza A and B viruses, and RSV were applied to all samples. While the tests identified numerous cases of SARS-CoV-2 and influenza A, and one case of influenza B, not a single RSV-positive result was detected across the entire patient cohort. Comparing these findings against previous studies that used polymerase chain reaction testing revealed a statistically significant deficit in RSV-positive cases, suggesting that rapid antigen testing substantially underdetects RSV in adults relative to the more sensitive PCR method. The authors conclude that rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests have limited diagnostic utility for RSV infection in Japanese adult patients.
items.citation: Kajiwara N, Tsunetoshi Y, Shiode S. No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients. 2026

items.title: Respiratory surveillance through point of care molecular testing and its impacts on nursing home RSV, influenza, and SARS-CoV-2 awareness and reporting behavior: a pilot study.
items.citation: Davidson H, Han L, McConeghy K et al.. Respiratory surveillance through point of care molecular testing and its impacts on nursing home RSV, influenza, and SARS-CoV-2 awareness and reporting behavior: a pilot study.. Antimicrobial stewardship & healthcare epidemiology : ASHE. 2026

items.title: Performance of an antigen test for detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza, and respiratory syncytial virus-a prospective study.
items.summary: This prospective study evaluated the diagnostic accuracy of a multiplex rapid antigen test designed to simultaneously detect SARS-CoV-2, influenza A, influenza B, and respiratory syncytial virus in patients presenting with acute respiratory infection symptoms. Over a thousand patients, including a subset of children, underwent both the nasal swab antigen test administered by emergency department staff and a separately collected nasopharyngeal PCR test at a central laboratory. The antigen test demonstrated high sensitivity for all four pathogens when benchmarked against PCR cycle threshold values indicative of higher viral loads, while specificity was perfect across all agents. The study also found that nasal swabs absorbed less viral RNA than nasopharyngeal swabs, a difference that was statistically significant for influenza A among adults but not apparent in children. The authors conclude that the test's performance across all age groups and viral targets supports its potential use as a marker of infectiousness in acute clinical care settings.
items.venue: Clinical microbiology and infection : the official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases
items.citation: Berger K, Vang SH, Bekkeseth RHO et al.. Performance of an antigen test for detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza, and respiratory syncytial virus-a prospective study.. Clinical microbiology and infection : the official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases. 2026

items.title: Analytical comparison of over-the-counter multiplex tests for influenza A, influenza B, and SARS-CoV-2.
items.summary: This study evaluated the comparative analytical performance of over-the-counter lateral flow tests designed to detect influenza A, influenza B, and SARS-CoV-2, filling a gap in consumer-facing performance data. Researchers prepared contrived specimen panels from propagated viral stocks of multiple strains in clinical matrix and applied them to eight commercially available tests, following manufacturer instructions with blinded result reading. The detection limits across all tests and all four viruses fell within a narrow fourfold dilution range, indicating broadly similar analytical sensitivity among the products evaluated. A notable finding was that at low viral concentrations, many tests appeared negative early in the interpretation window but converted to positive by the window's end. The authors therefore recommend that users wait for the full manufacturer-specified incubation period before concluding a result is truly negative.
items.citation: Lohsen S, Hoy-Schulz Y, Taing A et al.. Analytical comparison of over-the-counter multiplex tests for influenza A, influenza B, and SARS-CoV-2.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients
items.summary: The study investigated the burden of respiratory syncytial virus infection among adult outpatients in Japan by screening nasopharyngeal swabs from patients presenting with acute respiratory symptoms at a single clinic. Samples were tested using a rapid immunochromatographic antigen assay capable of simultaneously detecting RSV alongside influenza A, influenza B, and SARS-CoV-2. While the assay identified substantial numbers of influenza A, influenza B, and SARS-CoV-2 positive cases, no patients tested positive for RSV across the full collection period. Comparing these results against previous studies that used polymerase chain reaction testing revealed a statistically significant deficit in RSV detections, suggesting that cases were missed rather than absent. The authors conclude that rapid immunochromatographic antigen testing is insufficiently sensitive for diagnosing RSV infections in the adult Japanese population.
items.citation: Kajiwara N, Tsunetoshi Y, Shiode S. No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients. 2026

items.title: Scalable Visual Pretraining for Language Intelligence
items.summary: This paper challenges the prevailing assumption that large language models should be pretrained exclusively on text, arguing that visually rich documents contain information in figures, equations, and layouts that plain text conversion fails to capture. The authors conducted a systematic study of unsupervised visual pretraining paradigms that train directly on visual documents without extracting text. Testing across multiple model backbones and benchmarks, they found that visual pretraining on the same underlying corpora consistently outperforms text-only pretraining. The work proposes that visual pretraining represents a scalable and efficient alternative pathway for building foundation model intelligence.
items.citation: Yiming Zhang, Zhonghan Zhao, Wenwei Zhang et al.. Scalable Visual Pretraining for Language Intelligence. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models
items.summary: This paper investigated how vision-language models have progressed in describing complex visual scenes over roughly a decade, with particular attention to the types of errors these models make. The researchers introduced a new benchmark dataset depicting complex social interactions and behaviors, which they used alongside an existing simpler scene dataset to evaluate nine models spanning both pre- and post-multimodal large language model eras, comparing their outputs against human descriptions and a gold standard. They categorized errors into five visual-cognitive types, including object detection, recognition, hallucination, scene understanding, and spatial dependence. The study found that earlier models performed far below human-level accuracy on complex social scenes, whereas recent multimodal large language models have closed the gap with top-performing human describers and have largely eliminated the accuracy difference between simple and complex scene types. Nearly all error types have been substantially reduced in modern models, though spatial dependence errors persist, and detection, recognition, and hallucination errors were identified as having the greatest impact on overall description accuracy.
items.citation: Shravan Murlidaran, Miguel P. Eckstein. Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models. arXiv. 2026

items.title: VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents
items.summary: This paper investigated the use of large language model-based autonomous agents to perform vulnerability discovery and exploitation specifically in IoT environments, an application area that had not previously been explored. The authors developed a multi-agent framework called VEXAIoT, comprising a vulnerability detection agent and an attack execution agent that work together to conduct reconnaissance, plan attack sequences, and execute exploits against IoT services. The system was evaluated across ten attack scenarios mapped to OWASP IoT vulnerabilities within IoTGoat and Metasploitable testing environments. Across hundreds of attack executions, the framework achieved a very high overall success rate, with most attacks completing in under two minutes and low computational token overhead. The results demonstrate that LLM-driven agents can effectively automate IoT vulnerability assessment and offensive security workflows in controlled settings.
items.citation: Katherine Swinea, Kshitiz Aryal, Lopamudra Praharaj et al.. VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Spatial decomposition of Little Red Dots with JWST/NIRSpec IFU into broad-line red cores and narrow-line blue host galaxies
items.summary: This paper investigates Little Red Dots, a population of compact red sources discovered by JWST whose unusual spectral properties — including a V-shaped continuum, broad Balmer emission lines, and Balmer absorption — have debated physical origins. The researchers used JWST/NIRSpec integral field unit spectroscopy to spatially resolve and decompose the spectral components of five broad Hα-selected Little Red Dots at redshift approximately five, employing both prism and high-resolution grating observations. By mapping the spatial distribution of the blue and red continuum, narrow and broad emission lines, and absorption features, they found that the blue continuum is co-spatial with the narrow emission line region while the red continuum originates from a compact core co-spatial with the broad Balmer emission and absorption. A pronounced decrease in the equivalent width of [OIII] toward the central core further supports a two-component picture. The results provide spatial evidence that Little Red Dots consist of a red, compact central engine — likely hosting a rapidly growing black hole — embedded within a more extended blue host galaxy.
items.citation: Yuzo Ishikawa, Anna-Christina Eilers, Rohan P. Naidu et al.. Spatial decomposition of Little Red Dots with JWST/NIRSpec IFU into broad-line red cores and narrow-line blue host galaxies. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Lean-QIT: Towards a Formal Infrastructure for Quantum Information Theory
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of formally verifying quantum information theory within a machine-checked proof system, noting that existing frameworks lack a reusable operational layer that cleanly separates coding definitions from their information-theoretic characterizations. The authors developed Lean-QIT, a library built in Lean 4 that provides composable, kernel-verified interfaces for quantum states, channels, source and channel codes, finite-block performance criteria, hypothesis testing, one-shot quantities, and asymptotic rate constructions. Using this infrastructure, they formally verified several foundational theorems of quantum information theory, including Schumacher's quantum source-coding theorem, the Holevo–Schumacher–Westmoreland classical-capacity theorem, and the entanglement-assisted classical-capacity theorem along with its strong converse. By modularizing achievability, converse, and asymptotic components into reusable building blocks, the library creates a unified framework that connects finite-block protocols, analytic inequalities, and asymptotic limits within a single verified setting. The authors also position Lean-QIT as a foundation for AI-assisted formalization and automated proof search in quantum information and computation.
items.citation: Chengkai Zhu, Ziao Tang, Guocheng Zhen et al.. Lean-QIT: Towards a Formal Infrastructure for Quantum Information Theory. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Toward Real-Time Sentence-Level Sign Language Translation
items.summary: This paper investigates sentence-level sign language translation with the practical goal of real-time deployment on accessible hardware, rather than advancing translation architectures. The authors fine-tuned a SHuBERT-ByT5 model stack on a subset of the How2Sign dataset using parameter-efficient QLoRA training, keeping the sign encoding component frozen throughout. The system achieved modest translation quality scores on the test split and, more centrally, the authors designed a hardware-aware streaming pipeline that separates lightweight client devices from a compute-intensive backend, allowing the same backend to serve varied endpoints including a Raspberry Pi, browser, or phone. Through techniques such as chunked ingestion, bounded queues, parallelized perception, temporal reordering, and a sentence-boundary state machine, the system substantially reduced both mean and tail response latency compared to a baseline configuration. The primary contribution is thus an engineering framework enabling practical, low-latency sign language translation across heterogeneous client hardware rather than a novel modeling approach.
items.citation: Thanh-Hoang Nguyen Doan. Toward Real-Time Sentence-Level Sign Language Translation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: AI-Enhanced Point-of-Care Diagnostics for Infectious Diseases in Resource-Limited Settings: A Scoping Review.
items.summary: This scoping review systematically mapped the research landscape of artificial intelligence-enhanced point-of-care and rapid diagnostic technologies for infectious diseases in resource-limited settings, focusing on disease coverage, geographic representation, and validation quality. Following established scoping review methodology, the authors searched five major databases spanning over a decade of publications, screening over a thousand records and ultimately including more than two hundred studies. The field was found to be growing exponentially, with nearly half of all included studies appearing in the most recent period, and research dominated by a narrow triad of diseases — COVID-19, malaria, and tuberculosis — while neglected tropical diseases were substantially underrepresented. Convolutional neural networks were the most prevalent analytical approach, and microscopy and molecular diagnostics were the most common diagnostic modalities examined. Critically, the vast majority of studies lacked prospective field validation, geographic coverage was concentrated in East Africa and South Asia at the expense of West Africa and Latin America, and the authors conclude that the field must prioritize rigorous real-world validation, broader disease equity, and greater involvement of end-user communities to translate this growing body of research into meaningful clinical impact.
items.venue: Tropical medicine & international health : TM & IH
items.citation: Farquhar H. AI-Enhanced Point-of-Care Diagnostics for Infectious Diseases in Resource-Limited Settings: A Scoping Review.. Tropical medicine & international health : TM & IH. 2026

items.title: Point-of-care testing (POCT) for Infectious diseases: technological innovation, effectiveness evaluation, and application strategies in resource-limited regions.
items.summary: This review paper examines point-of-care testing technologies for infectious diseases, focusing on their role in early screening, epidemic surveillance, and public health prevention. The authors systematically surveyed recent technological advances across molecular diagnostics, microfluidic systems, biosensing technologies, and artificial intelligence as applied to rapid diagnostic tools. The paper also analyzes frameworks for evaluating the quality of these technologies using metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, detection limits, and field applicability. Particular emphasis is placed on how these technologies can be strategically deployed in resource-limited settings through optimized technical design, resource allocation, and local support infrastructure. The authors conclude by proposing future development directions intended to guide further research and practical implementation of infectious disease point-of-care testing globally.
items.citation: Zhang YX, Ru JF. Point-of-care testing (POCT) for Infectious diseases: technological innovation, effectiveness evaluation, and application strategies in resource-limited regions.. Diagnostic microbiology and infectious disease. 2026

items.title: Infectious disease diagnostic competencies of medical students as a fundamental basis for physicians' resilience in addressing future infectious disease challenges.
items.summary: This paper examines the role of infectious disease diagnostic competencies in medical education as a foundation for building long-term physician resilience against infectious disease challenges. The authors argue that structured, practice-oriented training in medical microbiology, virology, and hygiene — encompassing epidemiology, prevention, diagnostics, and therapy — is essential and cannot be replaced by self-directed learning formats. They emphasize that proficiency in rapid and appropriate infection diagnostics is a core competency underpinning rational antimicrobial therapy, which is critical for combating the development of multidrug-resistant pathogens. The paper further highlights the importance of skills such as correct sample collection, appropriate test selection, and critical evaluation of microbiological findings within the broader One-Health framework, which considers consequences for patients, society, animals, and ecosystems. The authors conclude that fostering these competencies through hands-on training during medical education is indispensable for outbreak control, interruption of infection chains, and sustainable preparedness for future infectious disease threats.
items.citation: Brandt C, Becker K, Ciesek S et al.. Infectious disease diagnostic competencies of medical students as a fundamental basis for physicians' resilience in addressing future infectious disease challenges.. GMS infectious diseases. 2026

items.title: From Control to Eradication: The Role of Point-of-Care Testing in Modernising Australia's Bovine Viral Diarrhoea (BVD) Disease Management.
items.summary: This review examines the current state of Bovine Viral Diarrhoea Virus management in Australia and evaluates how modern diagnostic approaches could transform national disease control efforts. The authors analyse the limitations of Australia's decentralised, non-compulsory biosecurity framework, which places responsibility on individual producers and results in fragmented, inconsistent disease management across beef and dairy systems. The review argues that rapid, field-deployable point-of-care diagnostics — including lateral flow assays, nucleic acid amplification tests, and biosensors — could serve as a pivotal tool for real-time identification and culling of persistently infected animals, which are the primary reservoir of transmission. The authors propose that integrating these technologies with strengthened biosecurity protocols and optimised vaccination strategies would provide a scientifically sound and commercially viable pathway to systematic national eradication, representing a shift from passive control to active elimination of the virus from Australian cattle industries.
items.citation: Ogada S, Naseem MN, Ranjbar S et al.. From Control to Eradication: The Role of Point-of-Care Testing in Modernising Australia's Bovine Viral Diarrhoea (BVD) Disease Management.. Viruses. 2026

items.title: Establishing an EU-compliant diagnostic facility for infectious diseases under war conditions in Poltava, Ukraine.
items.summary: This paper describes the establishment of advanced infectious disease diagnostic capabilities in a Ukrainian regional hospital during active wartime conditions. The authors implemented EU-compliant serological, automated PCR, and bacterial identification systems with antibiotic susceptibility testing at a regional infectious diseases hospital in Poltava, a city hosting a large internally displaced population that previously relied on low-sensitivity rapid tests. The initiative was structured as a German-Ukrainian hospital partnership featuring participatory equipment selection, hands-on staff training conducted in Germany, jointly developed standard operating procedures, and adaptive reagent supply chains. Over the course of the project, the laboratories conducted tens of thousands of tests, identifying previously unrecognized cases of HIV, hepatitis B and C, Lyme disease, toxoplasmosis, and antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections. The authors argue that this model demonstrates the feasibility of implementing high-quality diagnostic infrastructure under war conditions when partnership design emphasizes practical solutions, shared decision-making, and contingency planning, and propose it as a replicable template for other Ukrainian regions.
items.citation: Zelinskyy G, Koval T, Schrammel U et al.. Establishing an EU-compliant diagnostic facility for infectious diseases under war conditions in Poltava, Ukraine.. Frontiers in public health. 2026

items.title: Navigating the Future of Respiratory Infections: Key Insights From International Congress in Singapore, 17-20 September 2025.
items.summary: This paper summarizes the major themes and findings from an international conference focused on the prevention and management of respiratory infections. The authors reviewed plenary sessions, symposia, and panel discussions, synthesizing key concepts across therapeutics, host response, vaccination, surveillance, diagnostics, and research infrastructure. A central theme was the development of long-acting and broadly active antivirals, combination therapy strategies, and vaccines targeting conserved viral components, alongside growing recognition that host inflammatory responses play a substantial role in disease severity. The conference also emphasized integrated surveillance approaches incorporating genomic sequencing, metagenomics, and rapid diagnostics within a One Health framework to address zoonotic spillover risks. The authors conclude that clinical care, translational science, and public health preparedness are becoming increasingly interconnected, and that sustained investment across these domains is essential for future epidemic and pandemic readiness.
items.citation: Morvil N, Goh WGW, Zheng C et al.. Navigating the Future of Respiratory Infections: Key Insights From International Congress in Singapore, 17-20 September 2025.. Influenza and other respiratory viruses. 2026

items.title: SPOT-IC: Improving prediction for interval-censored data via survival probability transfer
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of making accurate survival predictions when data are interval-censored, a problem that is especially difficult under wide censoring intervals and limited follow-up as commonly seen in chronic disease research. The authors developed a novel transfer learning method called SPOT-IC that borrows survival probability information from external source studies to improve estimation in a target study, without requiring access to individual-level source data or imposing strict assumptions about similarity between source and target models. The method transfers information through a specially designed penalty term and uses an EM algorithm for efficient computation, while a data-adaptive aggregation procedure handles the case of multiple source studies with unknown informativeness, protecting against negative transfer. Theoretical results establish that the proposed estimator achieves a faster convergence rate than using only target data whenever at least one source study is sufficiently informative. Simulation studies and an application to Alzheimer's disease neuroimaging data confirm the practical effectiveness of the approach.
items.citation: Jingyi Hu, Yu Gu. SPOT-IC: Improving prediction for interval-censored data via survival probability transfer. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Thermalization in a Height-Conserving Quantum Dimer Model
items.summary: This paper investigates whether thermalization occurs within the constrained dynamics of a height-conserving quantum dimer model on the square lattice, where local plaquette flip moves conserve an emergent height field. The authors carefully resolve the model's symmetry sectors—including winding numbers, sublattice heights, and lattice momentum—to isolate the dominant connected components of the fragmented Hilbert space, then analyze spectral statistics, entanglement entropy, and connectivity within each sector. They find a striking decoupling between two standard diagnostics of quantum chaos: level-spacing statistics vary widely across momentum sectors, ranging from near-Poisson to Wigner-Dyson distributions, yet eigenstate entanglement entropy consistently collapses onto a narrow dome-shaped thermal curve across all sectors. Only a small number of low-entanglement outlier states break the otherwise uniform thermal pattern in select sectors. The study concludes that strong kinematic constraints can produce a form of constrained quantum chaos in which spectral correlations and eigenstate thermalization hypothesis signatures need not coincide, challenging the conventional expectation that these diagnostics go hand in hand.
items.citation: Junsheng Feng, Jie Ren, Zheng Yan. Thermalization in a Height-Conserving Quantum Dimer Model. arXiv. 2026

items.title: SAGEAgent: A Self-Evolving Agent for Cost-Aware Modality Acquisition in Multimodal Survival Prediction
items.summary: This paper investigates whether complete diagnostic workups are necessary for all cancer patients in survival prediction, framing the challenge as a sequential decision problem within multimodal clinical oncology. The authors propose SAGEAgent, a large language model-based clinical agent that reasons about which diagnostic modalities to acquire for each patient, respecting the clinically ordered workflow from low-burden inputs like demographics to high-burden ones like genomic profiling. The agent incorporates clinical tools that translate numerical predictions into text, an episodic memory for retrieving similar past cases, and a semantic memory that accumulates reusable decision patterns over time, enabling it to self-evolve with experience. Evaluated on a glioma cohort combining multiple datasets with four diagnostic modalities, SAGEAgent achieves competitive survival prediction accuracy while substantially reducing the average diagnostic acquisition burden compared to approaches that assume full modality availability or passively handle missing data.
items.citation: Chongyu Qu, Can Cui, Zhengyi Lu et al.. SAGEAgent: A Self-Evolving Agent for Cost-Aware Modality Acquisition in Multimodal Survival Prediction. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Deep Learning for Dynamic Programming with Recursive Utility Using First-order Conditions
items.summary: This paper introduces a deep learning algorithm called certainty-equivalent first-order learning (CEFOL) designed to solve discrete-time dynamic programming problems featuring recursive utility, a class of problems that is computationally challenging because nonlinear certainty equivalents appear in the Bellman equation and are difficult to evaluate directly. The approach addresses this difficulty by deploying separate neural networks to represent the certainty equivalent, value functions, policy functions, and Lagrange multipliers, using model-specific first-order and Karush-Kuhn-Tucker optimality conditions to construct residuals that guide training. A key feature of CEFOL is its ability to handle general equality and inequality constraints, including occasionally binding constraints, without requiring penalty functions or problem-specific reformulations. The algorithm is applied to several canonical problems including risk-sensitive and Epstein-Zin consumption-saving models, a robust-control problem, and a dynamic stochastic general equilibrium model with recursive preferences and stochastic volatility. Across these applications, out-of-sample solution accuracy is strong, with Bellman and optimality residuals generally in the range of ten-thousandths to thousandths, and the learned functions closely match benchmark solutions where available.
items.citation: Xianhua Peng, Wu Guo, Songyan Wang et al.. Deep Learning for Dynamic Programming with Recursive Utility Using First-order Conditions. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Diagnosing quantum reservoirs at scale based on expressivity and coverage
items.summary: This paper addresses a core challenge in quantum reservoir computing: evaluating the quality of a quantum reservoir without optimizing it, which is essential since performance depends entirely on the reservoir family chosen rather than any trained parameters. The authors introduce a scalable diagnostic framework with two complementary metrics—an order-statistics expressivity score that benchmarks a reservoir ensemble's largest output probabilities against a theoretical Haar random baseline without reconstructing full output distributions, and an effective rank measure of the feature matrix that captures how much input-dependent information is available for the classical readout layer. The expressivity score is notable for having computational cost that does not grow with Hilbert space dimension and includes a closed-form correction for depolarizing noise, making it directly applicable on real quantum hardware. The framework is validated against established complexity measures, under simulated noise, and on IBM quantum hardware across multiple quantum machine learning benchmarks. The authors find that the expressivity score reliably captures the intrinsic hierarchy among reservoir families while the effective rank determines whether that expressivity translates into practically usable predictive information.
items.citation: Laia Domingo, Oriol Balló-Gimbernat, Fernando Vilariño. Diagnosing quantum reservoirs at scale based on expressivity and coverage. arXiv. 2026

items.title: PesTwin: A modular agent-based framework for pest and vector population control
items.summary: PesTwin is an agent-based modelling framework designed to simulate genetic control technologies for pest and vector species across diverse ecological settings and deployment strategies. The framework captures stochastic demographic processes, species-specific life-history traits, heterogeneous dispersal, and temporal variation in resource availability within a unified computational environment, enabling comparison of approaches such as the sterile insect technique, Wolbachia-based interventions, and genetic control technologies. The authors validated the framework against published laboratory cage data from multiple genetic control systems spanning two insect species, demonstrating close agreement between simulated and observed population trajectories including replicate-to-replicate variability. They further demonstrated that validated models can be extended to spatially explicit, field-scale scenarios to explore how the timing, density, and spatial placement of releases influence population suppression and spread across heterogeneous landscapes. The overarching claim is that by enabling in silico testing of genetic control systems before field deployment, PesTwin can accelerate the path from laboratory construct to operational intervention by informing construct prioritisation, experimental design, release strategy, and regulatory evaluation.
items.citation: Andrea De Antoni, Giovanni Iacca, Andrea Pugliese et al.. PesTwin: A modular agent-based framework for pest and vector population control. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Universities are relying on AI-detection software to catch cheating. How well do the programs work?
items.why_notable: Universities are increasingly turning to AI-detection software to identify cheating, but the tools vary considerably in both method and effectiveness. The work draws attention by questioning whether institutions can actually rely on these services to fairly and accurately flag AI-generated writing.
items.citation: Anna McKie. Universities are relying on AI-detection software to catch cheating. How well do the programs work?. Nature. 2026

items.title: Deep-sea oddities and boatloads of other new species — June’s best science images
items.why_notable: The work is drawing attention because it showcases striking visual discoveries from the natural world, including unusual deep-sea creatures and a wealth of newly identified species, curated as standout scientific imagery from the past month.
items.citation: Miryam Naddaf, Dhruv Shenai. Deep-sea oddities and boatloads of other new species — June’s best science images. Nature. 2026

items.title: The rise of computer chips — and the race to control them
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it reframes the history of computing by looking beyond the familiar narrative of software and Silicon Valley entrepreneurship to examine the broader forces shaping the development and control of computer chips.
items.citation: Chris Stokel-Walker. The rise of computer chips — and the race to control them. Nature. 2026

items.title: Time to give hydration breaks the red card? What science says about keeping cool
items.why_notable: The work is drawing attention because it challenges the scientific basis for a widely used sports safety practice, arguing that poorly applied cooling breaks are actually eroding confidence in heat-health research itself.
items.citation: Harry Brown. Time to give hydration breaks the red card? What science says about keeping cool. Nature. 2026

items.title: China boosts prestigious grants for young scientists — will it ease competition?
items.why_notable: China's main science funding body is significantly expanding the number of grants available to young researchers, raising questions about whether the increase will meaningfully reduce the intense competition for funding. The move is drawing attention as a potential shift in how the country supports early-career scientists.
items.citation: Xiaoying You. China boosts prestigious grants for young scientists — will it ease competition?. Nature. 2026

items.title: Electrodeposited self-assembled molecules for perovskite photovoltaics
items.why_notable: The work draws attention because it introduces electrodeposition as a method for applying self-assembled molecules in perovskite solar cells, offering a potentially new route to fabricating these devices.
items.citation: Zhihui Xiong, Xuanang Luo, Fushen Tang et al.. Electrodeposited self-assembled molecules for perovskite photovoltaics. Nature. 2026

items.title: Author Correction: Synthesis of enantioenriched atropisomers by biocatalytic deracemization
items.summary: The abstract describes only a correction to a previously published article, noting the omission of a citation rather than presenting any scientific findings. There is insufficient scientific content here to summarize the research itself.
items.why_notable: This work is receiving attention because it describes a biocatalytic approach to producing enantioenriched atropisomers through deracemization, a challenging goal in asymmetric synthesis. The correction notice indicates the study was significant enough to warrant formal follow-up to ensure its findings are accurately represented in the literature.
items.citation: Casey B. Roos, S. Luke Schulert, Lara E. Zetzsche et al.. Author Correction: Synthesis of enantioenriched atropisomers by biocatalytic deracemization. Nature. 2026

items.title: Neutrino’s nursery found: the ‘Shadow Blaster’
items.summary: The abstract is too thin to summarize beyond its single repeated claim: a high-energy neutrino detected at a South Pole observatory has been traced back to a starburst galaxy active during an early period of the Universe's history.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it identifies the birthplace of a high-energy neutrino detected at the South Pole, tracing it to an ancient, star-forming galaxy from the early Universe. Pinpointing such a cosmic source helps resolve longstanding questions about where the most energetic neutrinos originate.
items.citation: Neutrino’s nursery found: the ‘Shadow Blaster’. Nature. 2026

items.title: Daily briefing: ‘Cyborg’ cockroaches breathe underwater with printed suit
items.why_notable: The work is drawing attention because researchers have equipped cockroaches with a printed suit enabling them to survive and explore underwater for extended periods, a notable advance in bio-hybrid robotics. The briefing also highlights concerns about fraudulent cancer research accumulating significant scientific influence through citations.
items.citation: Jacob Smith. Daily briefing: ‘Cyborg’ cockroaches breathe underwater with printed suit. Nature. 2026

items.title: Can you actually do a nine-to-five PhD? Nature readers weigh in
items.why_notable: The question of whether doctoral research can realistically fit within standard working hours has struck a nerve, prompting researchers to share their struggles with managing time during a PhD.
items.citation: Laura Woodrow. Can you actually do a nine-to-five PhD? Nature readers weigh in. Nature. 2026

items.title: Incoming US science academy chief vows to ‘double down’ on research
items.why_notable: The incoming leader of a prominent national scientific body is drawing attention by making a forceful public commitment to strengthening research at a time when science's societal role is under scrutiny. His warning that "a society that loses science loses the future" signals an intent to defend and expand scientific endeavor rather than retreat from it.
items.citation: Jenna Ahart. Incoming US science academy chief vows to ‘double down’ on research. Nature. 2026

items.title: Togetherness: How co-operation built the world
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it examines how symbiosis and cooperative relationships between organisms have been fundamental in shaping life on Earth. The framing highlights the broad significance of togetherness across the natural world, suggesting that collaboration, rather than competition alone, is a driving force in biological history.
items.citation: Benjamin Thompson. Togetherness: How co-operation built the world. Nature. 2026

items.title: Backreaction of stimulated Hawking radiation in an optical analogue
items.why_notable: This work draws attention because it provides both theoretical and experimental evidence for the fundamental mechanism behind Hawking radiation and its backreaction effect, using a fiber-optical analogue of black hole physics to probe phenomena that are otherwise inaccessible to direct observation.
items.citation: Lorenzo M. Procopio, Raul Aguero-Santacruz, David Bermúdez et al.. Backreaction of stimulated Hawking radiation in an optical analogue. Nature. 2026

items.title: Harmonizing standards and resources for the medical genome
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it identifies fragmentation and inconsistency in how genomic sequencing is applied across clinical settings, and proposes that centralized reference standards are needed to advance the reliability of precision medicine, including emerging genetic therapies.
items.citation: Euan A. Ashley, Ash A. Alizadeh, Hanae Armitage et al.. Harmonizing standards and resources for the medical genome. Nature. 2026

items.title: Towards the construction of a virtual yeast
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because researchers have developed an artificial intelligence-driven agent capable of modelling the cellular behaviours of a eukaryotic organism, representing a significant step toward a comprehensive computational simulation of a living cell.
items.citation: Liujia Qian, Zizhuo Zhou, Peijie Zhou et al.. Towards the construction of a virtual yeast. Nature. 2026

items.title: Isomeric multi-hydrogen-bonding enables blue perovskite LEDs
items.why_notable: Blue perovskite LEDs have long lagged behind their red and green counterparts in both efficiency and color purity, making this a critical bottleneck for full-color displays. By engineering isomeric molecules to create multi-hydrogen-bonding networks, this work achieves record external quantum efficiencies alongside stable, saturated blue emission, representing a significant advance toward practical perovskite-based display technology.
items.citation: Yuanzhi Wang, Chengxi Zhang, Yingguo Yang et al.. Isomeric multi-hydrogen-bonding enables blue perovskite LEDs. Nature. 2026
